Overall Outcome Progress Radar

Savings Transformation
+48.8%
average improvement across savings rates, VSLA participation, and loan access. Personal saving (43.1%→93.4%), VSLA participation (43.6%→98%), women taking loans (28%→79.1%).
Disaster Preparedness
+35%
average improvement across preparedness knowledge, action knowledge, and early warning access. From 4.7%→37.8%, 11.8%→46.3%, 8.9%→45.0%.
Climate Awareness
+45%
average improvement across climate awareness and NbS understanding. Climate change awareness: 60.5%→96.3%, NbS solutions understanding: 23.1%→77.8%.
Women Empowerment
+24%
average improvement across 5 key household decisions: market access (+34.6pp), mangrove work (+39.3pp), routine purchases (+25pp), business/loans (+19.2pp), child education (+1.6pp).
NbS Participation
+45%
average improvement across conservation activities. Mangrove restoration: 36.9%→86.5%, Seaweed farming: 19.8%→74.7%, Forest management: 67.4%→98.4%.
Life Skills Growth
+26%
average improvement across self-esteem (5.9%-19.6%), aspirations (9%-18%), leadership (31%-37.4%), and communication skills (15%-35%).
Women Taking Loans
79.1%
↑ 51.1pp from 28.0% baseline
VSLA Participation
98.0%
↑ 54.4pp from 43.6% baseline
Climate Knowledge
96.3%
↑ 35.8pp from 60.5% baseline
Preparedness Training
80.6%
↑ 34.5pp from 11.8% baseline
Joint Decisions
58.8%
↑ 13.8pp from 45.0% baseline
Household Electricity
19.4%
↓ 8.3pp from 27.7% baseline

COSME Logframe Indicator Performance

Midline achievement against official M&E targets. Exceptional performance across 8 core indicators: 7 out of 8 EXCEEDED targets. Strongest gains in decision-making involvement (1200a: 57.6%→95.9%), NbS knowledge (1210a: 20.7%→80.9%), savings groups (1220a: 28.8%→91.9%), and clean energy access (1220b: 50.8%→83.1%). Unpaid work time reduced below target (1200b: 9.3→7.7 hours). Gender equality perception improved significantly (1210c: 42.1→64.7/100). One indicator (1210b: skills & confidence at 46.3%) is on track but has not yet reached the 55% target.

Indicator Baseline Target Midline Achievement Status
INTERMEDIATE OUTCOME - Behavior & Practice Change (1200-series)
1200a. Percentage of targeted women with adequate involvement in HH, group and enterprise decision making 57.6% 65.0% 95.9% +38.3pp EXCEEDED
1200b. Average time women spend in unpaid work 9.3 hours 8.3 hours 7.7 hours -1.6 hrs EXCEEDED
1200c. Percentage of targeted women with adequate access and control over household and enterprise resources 41.2% 50.0% 53.8% +12.6pp EXCEEDED
IMMEDIATE OUTCOME - Knowledge, Skills & Assets (1210/1220-series)
1210a. Women with adequate knowledge on NbS, Economic Rights & GE&I 20.70% 55.00% 80.9% +60.2pp EXCEEDED
1210b. Women with adequate skills & confidence to assert rights & express decisions 32.70% 55.00% 46.3% +13.6pp ON TRACK
1210c. Degree/extent women perceived as equal to men by targeted women 42.1/100 55.0/100 64.7/100 +22.6 pts EXCEEDED
1220a. Women actively saving in a Savings Group 28.80% 70.00% 91.9% +63.1pp EXCEEDED
1220b. Women with access to time-saving & environment-friendly solar & cooking stoves 50.80% 80.00% 83.1% +32.3pp EXCEEDED
A. HOUSEHOLD CHARACTERISTICS

Key Demographic Shifts

Female-headed households increased from 51.0% to 57.7%, reflecting widowhood (11.2% → 20.6%). This indicates higher vulnerability requiring targeted support. Education levels remain relatively stable with 45.9% having primary education, suggesting need for adult literacy programs.

Women by Location/Intervention Type
Marine sites (57.2%) show better environmental alignment with coastal conservation activities. Terrestrial sites declining may indicate migration patterns.
Household Headship Shifts
Female-headed households increased significantly, indicating vulnerability increases. Targeted gender-responsive programming critical.
Primary Economic Activities - Transformation
Major shift: Small business increased from 17.6% to 30.3%, while casual labour decreased 25.5% → 11.6%. Success indicator: Economic diversification moving women toward more stable income sources.
Housing Quality Indicator
Natural floor materials increased 66.6% → 79.4%, indicating persistent vulnerability. Infrastructure investment needed alongside livelihood support.
Water & Sanitation Access
Water access declined 84.3% → 79.7%, attributed to drought impacts. Toilet facilities improved slightly to 72.5%, still leaving 27.5% without adequate facilities.
B. SHOCKS, STRESSES & COPING STRATEGIES

Critical Finding: High Shock Exposure

63.4% of households still experiencing drought (down from 69.1%), 22.8% experiencing heat/cold waves (up from 8.6%), and 10% experiencing floods. Heat stress emerging as new threat requiring adaptation strategies.

Primary Shock Types Experienced
Drought remains dominant shock. Heat/cold waves and flooding increasing, indicating climate variability intensifying. Preparedness investments paying off - women better equipped to respond.
Shock Impact on Household Wellbeing
Positive trend: Women reporting "very large extent" impact decreased 32.7% → 20%, indicating improved resilience and coping mechanisms.
Coping Strategies - From Crisis to Resilience
Positive shifts: Took loans increased 4.7% → 25.8% (financial services access), Used savings increased 11.2% → 15.8% (program success).
Concern: Skipped meals still at 43.8% - continued food security vulnerability despite interventions.
C. DISASTER PREPAREDNESS & EARLY WARNING SYSTEMS

PROGRAM CORNERSTONE: Remarkable Preparedness Achievement

Knowledge of preparedness plans skyrocketed from 4.7% to 37.8%. Women knowing what to do in emergencies increased 11.8% → 46.3%. Access to early warning information went from 8.9% to 45.0% - representing core program delivery on DRR.

Knowledge of Community Preparedness Plans
From 50.1% not knowing to only 20% is a transformational shift. Program communication strategies highly effective. Continued engagement critical for retention.
Women Participating in Plan Development
26.9% now directly participate in preparedness planning - up from only 1.7%. Inclusive planning ensuring local context integration.
Access to Weather & Early Warning Information
Transformational: Direct weather forecast access jumped 15.7% → 46.2%. Early warning access 8.9% → 45.0%. Information accessibility critical for response decisions.
D. ACCESS & CONTROL OVER PRODUCTIVE RESOURCES

Asset Control Dynamics - Sole Women Ownership

Strong improvements in women's sole ownership of key productive assets. Cellphones surged 47.0% → 62.5%, the strongest asset control gain. Cooking stoves increased 3.5% → 39.7%, indicating high receptivity to clean energy. Solar panels improved 9.2% → 31.6%, reflecting renewable energy expansion. Furniture (household durables) increased modestly 17.3% → 23.1%. These gains demonstrate increasing women's agency over productive resources, though furniture shows women maintaining control of fewer household items - reflecting economic prioritization.

Key Asset Ownership Changes - Women's Control
Strong sole ownership gains: Cellphones 47.0% → 62.5% (+15.5pp), Cooking stoves 3.5% → 39.7% (+36.2pp), Solar panels 9.2% → 31.6% (+22.4pp). Sustainable assets: Women now control majority of cooking stoves - clean energy success achieved. Cellphones dominant asset. Furniture modest gain 17.3% → 23.1%.
Agricultural Land Status
Owned land decreased from 92.7% to 73.4%, while leased increased 6.0% → 19.6%. Tenure security declining - undermines investment incentives. Policy engagement needed.
Agricultural Land Under Cultivation
Agricultural land utilization at midline: 80.4% of available land under productive use. Combined with land access challenges, suggests shifting from agriculture to other income sources aligns with economic activity changes noted in livelihood diversification.
Availability of Key Agricultural Inputs
Excellent improvements across all inputs: Fertilizers (84.7% → 98.4%), Seeds (93.5% → 99.4%), Pesticides (93.1% → 98.7%), Veterinary drugs (73.6% → 87.5%), Animal feed (88.3% → 95.1%). Input supply chain effectively functioning - agricultural production constrained by demand shifts and land access, not input availability.
E. SAVINGS, LOANS & FINANCIAL INCLUSION

FLAGSHIP SUCCESS: Savings Revolution Through VSLA

Personal savings nearly doubled: 43.1% → 93.4%. VSLA participation expanded dramatically from 43.6% to 98.0% - representing nearly universal financial group membership. Weekly savings became dominant pattern: 52.6% → 91.9%. Women's access to credit surged: 28.0% → 79.1% taking loans personally. Average loan size decreased (indicating better repayment distribution), suggesting loan products better aligned with livelihood needs.

Women Personally Saving Behavior
116% increase in savers. Habit formation successful. Weekly saving pattern (91.9%) indicates sustainable practice adoption.
Savings Mechanism - VSLA Dominance
Transformational: VSLA jumped from 43.6% to 98.0%. Bank/formal institutions declining, indicating group mechanism superior for this population.
Women Taking Personal Loans
182% increase in women accessing credit. Credit dependency through VSLA (93.3%) supports sustainability - group dynamics ensure accountability and mutual support.
Intended Use of Savings - Shifting to Productive Assets
Strategic shift: Food purchases declining (46.2% → 28.4%), Large assets increasing (10.2% → 63.9%), Agricultural investment (10.9% → 30.4%). Savings transitioning from consumption to productive investment - indicating business mindset development.
F. ROLES, RESPONSIBILITIES & TIME POVERTY

TRANSFORMATIONAL PROGRESS: Time Poverty & Task Distribution Shift

Time workload improved: Unpaid care work reduced from 9.3 to 7.7 hours daily (17% reduction). Behavioral breakthrough: Baseline showed 87% of women doing cooking, 85% cleaning, 77% water fetching, 83% firewood collection alone. Midline shows dramatic shifts to 29%, 33%, 22%, 37% respectively - representing 50-60 percentage point reductions. Norms transformation underpins behavior change: Belief that cooking should be joint responsibility surged from 12.5% to 71.3%, providing social foundation for actual task redistribution. Women's aspirations and reality finally converging - demonstrating that norm messaging translates to concrete household practice changes when combined with livelihood security.

Time Investment in Daily Activities (Hours) - Reduction Through Program
Unpaid care work reduced 1.6 hours (17% decrease). Productive work increased 0.7 hours, positive reallocation. Still challenging - women averaging 12.6 hours of work daily.
Beliefs: Should be Joint Responsibility (% agreeing)
Norms shifting significantly - 71% believe cooking should be joint, up from 12.5%. However, women still performing task at baseline 87% of time - implementation gap between attitude and behavior narrowing through program implementation.
Actual Experience: Women Performing These Tasks
Significant progress: Household task distribution improved dramatically from baseline. Baseline showed 83% performing firewood, 85% cleaning, 87% cooking. Midline reduced to 37%, 33%, 29% respectively - indicating successful norm shift toward joint responsibility. Continued engagement needed to sustain gains.
Productive Work Participation - Women's Activity
Market selling increased 23.7% → 47.8%, seaweed farming 1.3% → 12.8%. Women successfully engaging in income activities despite time pressures through strategic livelihood diversification.
G. HOUSEHOLD & COMMUNITY DECISION MAKING

Women's Voice Growing in Household Decisions

Joint decision-making on all major issues increased 15-34% across 11 decision types. Women's influence on savings decisions, business investments, and loan decisions improved most (16-17% increases). This is reinforced by declining harmful social norms — "Man's job to earn, woman's to care for home" fell from 48.2% → 22.7% (-25.4pp), and "Husband should handle woman's income" declined 24.4% → 15.9% (-8.4pp). Mangrove/seaweed work influence jumped 17% → 51.9% - reflecting program's success in positioning these as female-led activities.

Joint Decision Making Increases Across Key Household Decisions
Strongest gains:Mangrove/seaweed work (9.8% → 49.1%), Routine purchases (25.6% → 50.6%), Market access (13.5% → 48.1%). Improving across board, indicating household gender relations shifting.
Women's Influence on Decisions - Large Extent
Mangrove/seaweed work influence tripled 17% → 51.9% (program focus areas). Savings and investment decisions improved 16% + increases. Women perceiving greater voice in community conservation decisions.
Comparison - Should vs Actually Making Decisions
Gap exists between beliefs and practice across all decisions. Women want more joint decisions than they're experiencing. Husband engagement and norm operationalization require continued focus.
H. CLIMATE CHANGE KNOWLEDGE & NATURE-BASED SOLUTIONS

TRANSFORMATIONAL LEARNING: Climate & NbS Concept Adoption

Climate change awareness exploded 60.5% → 96.3%. Understanding NbS solutions increased 23.1% → 77.8%. Women providing NbS examples increased 22.9% → 77.2%. Mangrove restoration knowledge 20.9% → 60.7%, and active participation 36.9% → 86.5%. Seaweed farming participation 19.8% → 74.7%. Forest management involvement 67.4% → 98.4%. Program successfully transformed indigenous conservation knowledge into modern framing of climate action.

Climate Change Awareness & Understanding
59% increase in awareness. Definition understanding improved (Completely Acceptable: 41% → 55.2%), with very few unable to answer now.
Climate Change Effects Experienced - Perception
Increased drought recognition (54.7% → 75.9%), hotter temperatures (39.5% → 67.5%), increased water insecurity perception (32.7% → 62.8%). Women connecting climate phenomena to livelihood impacts.
Nature-Based Solutions Knowledge & Participation
Mangrove restoration: active participation 36.9% → 86.5%. Interested in continuing: 58.8% → 86.6%. Seaweed farming participation 19.8% → 74.7%. Forest management 67.4% → 98.4%. Program successfully mobilized women into conservation workforce.
Perceived Benefits of NbS Solutions
Massive shifts: Carbon capture 2.8% → 33.1%, Air quality 11.5% → 47.2%, Water conservation 9.3% → 35.9%. Women understanding multiple benefits beyond biodiversity - ecosystem services awareness excellent learning outcome.
I. LIFE SKILLS & PSYCHOSOCIAL EMPOWERMENT

Confidence & Agency Transformation

Self-esteem across all dimensions improved 8.7%-19.6%. Women respecting themselves increased 78.2% → 97.8%. Leadership capacity jumped 59.1% → 94.1%. Women bringing people together for initiatives 60.9% → 92.5%. Communication comfort with community members increased 52.5% → 87.2%. Life skills programming successfully shifted mindsets from victimhood to agency and leadership.

Self-Esteem & Confidence - Percentage Agreeing/Strongly Agreeing
Across all self-esteem measures 5.9%-19.6% improvements. Foundation for other outcomes - women believing in themselves more likely to pursue opportunities.
Aspirations & Goal-Oriented Thinking
Women thinking about future 88.2% → 97.5%, having specific goals 81.2% → 99.1%, knowing how to achieve goals 72.1% → 96.9%. Planning capacity dramatically strong.
Leadership Capacity - Community Influence
Leadership ability 59.1% → 94.1% (+35%), bringing people together 60.9% → 92.5% (+31.6%), others seeking advice 54.8% → 92.2% (+37.4%). Women becoming community mobilizers and change agents.
Communication Skills - Comfort & Effectiveness
All communication measures improved 15-35%. Women more comfortable expressing views with family (75.8% → 95.6%) and community (52.5% → 87.2%). Others understanding them better (59.6% → 90.0%).
J. SOCIAL NORMS & GENDER ATTITUDES

Strong Positive Norm Shifts Across All Dimensions

Major progress: Agreement with harmful social norms declined significantly across all 10 statements. Largest declines: "Only men should drive boats" dropped 64.9% → 9.1% (-55.8pp), "Man's job to earn, woman's to care for home" fell 48.2% → 22.7% (-25.4pp), "Cook/clean = good wife" dropped 65.4% → 40.3% (-25.1pp). Harassment norms: "Dressed inappropriately = her fault" declined 55.1% → 33.1% (-22.0pp). Conservation norms: "Planting seeds is women's work" fell 17.3% → 5.6% (-11.7pp). Strong agreement also declined substantially, with "Man earn/woman home" dropping 12.5% → 1.9% (-10.6pp) and "Only men drive boats" falling 15.6% → 4.1% (-11.5pp). Norm transformation is real and deep.

Social Norms Dynamics - Gender Role Beliefs (Agree + Strongly Agree)
Major positive shifts: "Only men drive boats" plummeted 64.9% → 9.1% (-55.8pp). "Man earn/woman home" fell 48.2% → 22.7% (-25.4pp). "Cook/clean = good wife" dropped 65.4% → 40.3% (-25.1pp). All 10 harmful norm statements declined significantly — norm transformation is deep and consistent.
Gender Role Attitudes - Strong Agreement Only
Strong agreement with harmful norms declined substantially. "Man earn/woman home" dropped 12.5% → 1.9% (-10.6pp), "Only men drive boats" fell 15.6% → 4.1% (-11.5pp), "Cook/clean = good wife" declined 17.3% → 8.4% (-8.8pp). Only exception: "Women display emotions OK but not men" slightly increased 4.2% → 6.3% (+2.0pp).
Environment & Conservation Gender Norms
Conservation gender norms declining positively. "Planting seeds is women's work" fell 17.3% → 5.6% (-11.7pp). "Ecosystem restoration is women's work" declined 12.0% → 7.5% (-4.5pp). "Embarrassing for men to do domestic chores" dropped 31.7% → 10.6% (-21.1pp). Communities increasingly rejecting gendered divisions of environmental and domestic labor.
Household Management & Money Control Beliefs
"Husband should handle woman's income" declined 24.4% → 15.9% (-8.4pp). "Men have better business ideas" fell 25.8% → 15.9% (-9.8pp). Women increasingly rejecting male financial control norms, reflecting growing confidence in their own financial and business capabilities.

Men Survey - COSME Project

Parallel Analysis: Male Household Members & Community Leaders
Sample Size
267 Men
Survey Period
Baseline to Midline
Data Quality
High Confidence

Community Forest Conservation Groups

Forest Functionality Assessment: Baseline to Midline Analysis
Groups Assessed
28 Groups
Survey Period
Baseline to Midline
Data Quality
High Confidence

Indicator 1100a: Area (hectares) under rehabilitation using gender responsive nature-based solutions for climate mitigation and adaptation with biodiversity benefits, disaggregated by terrestrial, aquatic and coastal ecosystem

Baseline
0
Target
123.8 ha
Midline Result
63 ha
Change
+63 ha
Progress
50.9% of Target
A. OVERVIEW & FUNCTIONALITY SCORES

Functionality Assessment Summary

Overall group functionality improved significantly from baseline (n=43) to midline (n=28). Groups meeting the ≥60% threshold increased from 81.4% to 96.4% (+15.0pp). Groups meeting the ≥70% threshold surged from 53.5% to 85.7% (+32.2pp). Overall score improved from 70.6 to 84.2 (+13.6pp), with the strongest gains in Gender domain (+52.6pp).

Functionality Threshold — Groups Meeting ≥60% & ≥70% Benchmarks
Groups meeting ≥60% threshold: 81.4%→96.4% (+15.0pp). Groups meeting ≥70% threshold: 53.5%→85.7% (+32.2pp). Strong upward trajectory across both benchmarks.
Domain Scores — Management, Gender & Effectiveness
Management: 78.6%→78.0% (−0.6pp). Gender: 32.9%→85.5% (+52.6pp — strongest gain). Effectiveness: 72.0%→90.1% (+18.1pp). Overall: 70.6%→84.2% (+13.6pp).
Functionality Score Distribution — Average, Max & Min (Baseline n=43, Midline n=28)
Average score improved from 70.6 to 84.2 (+13.6). Maximum improved from 90.4 to 98.5. Minimum lifted from 37.0 to 46.7, indicating weakest groups are also improving.
B. GROUP CHARACTERISTICS

Group Profile Summary

Average group membership grew from 27 to 31 members, with female membership increasing from 19 to 23 (21% growth). Male membership averaged 7→8 members. Most groups operate in the 0-3 year range at baseline (34.9%) shifting slightly at midline (37.0%).

Average Group Membership — Male vs Female
Male: 7→8 (+14.3%). Female: 19→23 (+21.1%). Total: 27→31 (+14.8%). Female membership growing faster than male.
Group Affiliations — AMCOS, Beekeepers & Wellness
AMCOS: 42.5%→52.5% (+10.0pp). Beekeepers: 27.5%→35.0% (+7.5pp). Wellness: 15.0%→25.0% (+10.0pp). All affiliations growing.
Years of Operation — Distribution
Years distribution: 0-3 yrs: 34.9%→37.0%. 4-5 yrs: 30.2%→22.2%. 6-10 yrs: 20.9%→29.6%. 11+ yrs: 14.0%→11.1%.
Legal Registration Status
Registered groups increased from 72.5% to 85.0% (+12.5pp). Unregistered decreased 27.5%→15.0%. Formalization is progressing well.
Forest Size Distribution (Hectares)
Forest size distribution: 0 ha: 14.0%→0%. 1-5 ha: 30.2%→71.4%. 11-100 ha: 23.3%→7.1%. Midline shows concentration in 1-5 ha range.
Communities Near Forest — Distribution
More communities engaging: 7-10 communities increased 15.0%→20.0%. Groups with 1-3 communities declined 45.0%→35.0%, suggesting broader reach.
C. OBJECTIVES, RIGHTS & GOVERNANCE

Governance Improvements

Groups with fully written objectives already high at baseline: 95.3%→100%. Board roles well-defined & understood improved from 67.4% to 92.9%. Regular meetings with full quorum at 95.3%→96.4%. Women in significant leadership positions increased from 69.8% to 89.3%. Management practices (Agree+SA) remain strong above 82% across all 6 indicators.

Groups with Objectives in Written Format
Written objectives: No 2.3%→0%, Partially 2.3%→0%, Yes 95.3%→100%. Near-universal objective documentation.
Board/Committee Roles Definition & Understanding
Well-defined & understood: 67.4%→92.9% (+25.5pp). Not defined at all: 2.3%→3.6%. Governance clarity strengthening.
Meeting Regularity & Attendance
Regular/Full quorum: 95.3%→96.4% (+1.1pp). Occasional/Low: 2.3%→0%. Meeting participation consistently strong.
Women in Leadership Positions
Significant leadership: 69.8%→89.3% (+19.5pp). No leadership/no voice: 0%→0%. Strong female leadership in governance.
Management Practices — Agree + Strongly Agree (%)
Transparent elections: 95.3%→89.3%. Inclusive decisions: 97.7%→89.3%. Grievance mechanism: 97.7%→82.1%. Benefit sharing: 88.4%→92.9%. All indicators remain high (>82%).
D. TRAINING & ASSETS

Training & Resource Mobilization

Training coverage improved substantially: groups where "Most Members" received training grew from 11.6% to 71.4% (+59.8pp). Groups receiving no training dropped from 67.4% to 10.7%. Assets/inputs received increased from 18.6% to 78.6%. Key training topics include Climate Change & Forests (84%), CFM Concepts (52%), and Gender & Governance (52%).

Training Coverage — Member Participation Levels
Most members trained: 11.6%→71.4% (+59.8pp). No training: 67.4%→10.7% (-56.7pp). Training reach expanding dramatically.
Top Training Topics Received (%)
Climate Change & Forests: 57.1%→84.0%. CFM Concepts: 35.7%→52.0%. Forest Livelihoods: 21.4%→44.0%. Gender & Governance: 0%→52.0%.
Assets & Inputs Received
Groups receiving assets: 18.6%→78.6% (+60.0pp). Seedlings/nursery most common (63.6% at midline), followed by tools & equipment (77.3%) and water & irrigation (40.9%).
Asset Types Received by Groups (%)
Seedlings/Nursery: 4.7%→63.6%. Tools & Equipment: 9.3%→77.3%. Beekeeping: 2.3%→27.3%. Energy (Solar/Stoves): 0%→40.9%.
E. GENDER EQUALITY

Gender Equality Progress

Groups discussing gender equality increased from 23.3% to 96.4% (+73.1pp). Groups taking gender equality actions improved from 39.5% to 92.9%. Key topics discussed include equitable roles/responsibilities (96.3%), women's leadership & voice (81.5%), and resource sharing (55.6%). Gender action completion ("All actions") changed from 76.5% to 42.3%.

Groups Discussing Gender Equality
Yes: 23.3%→96.4% (+73.1pp). No: 76.7%→3.6%. Gender equality conversations now near-universal in forest groups.
Gender Equality Topics Discussed (%)
Equitable roles: 60.0%→96.3%. Resource sharing: 30.0%→55.6%. Women leadership & voice: 50.0%→81.5%. Strong gains across all topics.
Gender Equality Actions Taken
Yes (fully): 39.5%→92.9% (+53.4pp). No: 53.5%→7.1% (-46.4pp). Partially: 7.0%→0%. Groups moving decisively from discussion to action.
Gender Action Completion Level
All actions completed: 76.5%→42.3%. Many: 23.5%→50.0%. None: 0%→0%. Action completion shifting from "all" to "many" as scope of actions expanded.
F. EFFECTIVENESS & FOREST CONSERVATION

Conservation Outcomes

Forest condition rated "Good" varied across characteristics: Area 39.5%→42.9%, Biodiversity 39.5%→50.0%, Ecosystem Services 39.5%→39.3%. Threat levels were generally low: Fire (High) at 2.3%→0%, Charcoal (High) at 9.3%→7.1%. Income-generating groups increased from 46.5% to 53.6%. Agroforestry adoption grew from 55.8% to 60.7%.

Forest Condition — "Good" Rating Baseline vs Midline (%)
Area: 39.5%→42.9%. Volume/Biomass: 44.2%→25.0%. Regeneration: 44.2%→42.9%. Biodiversity: 39.5%→50.0%. Ecosystem Services: 39.5%→39.3%.
Forest Condition Breakdown — Baseline (Good / Medium / Poor)
Baseline: Good ratings range 39.5–44.2%. Medium ratings 25.6–37.2%. Poor ratings 23.3–30.2%. Volume/Biomass and Regeneration show highest Good ratings.
Forest Condition Breakdown — Midline (Good / Medium / Poor)
Midline: Good ratings range 25.0–50.0%. Medium ratings 35.7–50.0%. Poor ratings 7.1–25.0%. Biodiversity improved to 50.0% Good rating.
Forest Status Changes — Baseline (Decrease / No Change / Increase)
Baseline: Increase dominant across characteristics (55.8–65.9%). No Change ranges 16.3–27.9%. Decrease observed in 9.8–23.3% of groups.
Forest Status Changes — Midline (Decrease / No Change / Increase)
Midline: Decrease reduced (7.1–10.7%). No Change ranges 11.1–28.6%. Increase grew to 64.3–81.5%, showing strong positive conservation trajectory.
Forest Status — Perceived Increase Baseline vs Midline (%)
Groups perceiving increase: Area 60.5%→64.3%, Volume 60.5%→71.4%, Regeneration 55.8%→81.5%, Biodiversity 60.5%→81.5%, Ecosystem 65.9%→71.4%.
Forest Threats — "High" Level Baseline vs Midline (%)
Fire: 2.3%→0% (-2.3pp). Charcoal: 9.3%→7.1% (-2.2pp). Encroachment: 11.6%→3.6% (-8.0pp). Threat levels already low at baseline.
Threats Breakdown — Baseline (Low / Medium / High)
Baseline: High threats low overall. Fuelwood highest at 16.3%, Encroachment and Land Grabbing at 11.6%. Most threats predominantly Low level (65–93%).
Threats Breakdown — Midline (Low / Medium / High)
Midline: High threats further reduced (0–7.1%). Low threats dominant (71.4–89.3%). Medium levels stable. Continued low threat environment.
Threat Changes — Perceived Decrease Baseline vs Midline (%)
Groups perceiving threat decrease: Fire 48.8%→66.7%, Charcoal 48.8%→75.0%, Fuelwood 42.9%→67.9%. More groups reporting threat reduction at midline.
Income Generation & Agroforestry Adoption
Income-generating: 46.5%→53.6% (+7.1pp). Agroforestry adopted: 55.8%→60.7% (+4.9pp). Moderate economic growth observed.
Harvest Products — "Substantial" Amount (%)
NWFPs: 7.0%→3.6%. Food: 9.3%→3.6%. Woodfuel: 2.3%→0% (declining — positive conservation signal). Timber: 0%→0% (no extraction). Substantial harvesting minimal overall.
Agroforestry Practices & Objectives (%)
Top practices: Intercropping 83.3%→82.4%, Beekeeping 8.3%→23.5%, Alley Cropping 20.8%→0%. Top objectives: Biodiversity, soil fertility, income diversification.

Training Pre & Post Test Results

Knowledge Assessment: Pre-Training vs Post-Training Analysis
Pre-Test Participants
546
Post-Test Participants
909
Passing Threshold
≥70%
A. PASSING THRESHOLDS & OVERALL SCORES

Knowledge Assessment Summary — Forest Conservation Training

Participants completing training demonstrated significant knowledge gains across all thresholds and domains. Those meeting the ≥70% passing threshold surged from 50.0% to 94.7% (+44.7pp). Average scores improved from 65.2% to 85.4% (+20.2pp). All 21 knowledge topics showed improvement, with the strongest gains in Forest Ecosystems (+34.9pp), Climate Adaptation (+21.9pp), and Sustainable Harvesting (+26.1pp).

Passing Threshold — Participants Meeting ≥50%, ≥60%, ≥70%, ≥80% Benchmarks
≥50%: 76.4%→97.7% (+21.3pp). ≥60%: 64.8%→96.6% (+31.8pp). ≥70%: 50.0%→94.7% (+44.7pp). ≥80%: 32.9%→85.7% (+52.8pp). Strongest gain at ≥80% threshold.
Overall Scores — Average, Maximum & Minimum (%)
Average: 65.2%→85.4% (+20.2pp). Maximum stable at 100%. Minimum improved from 2.5% to 27.5% (+25.0pp), lifting the lowest performers significantly.
B. FOREST ECOLOGY & RIGHTS

Forest Ecology & Rights: Foundational Knowledge

Topics covering core forest ecosystems, biodiversity importance, community forestry principles, forest rights, conservation fundamentals, and tree species identification. All 6 topics showed significant post-training improvement, with Forest Ecosystems showing the strongest gain (+34.9pp) and Community Forestry the highest post-test score (97.7%).

Forest Ecology & Rights — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Performance (%)
Forest Ecosystems: 56.6%→91.5% (+34.9pp). Biodiversity: 85.7%→97.6% (+11.9pp). Community Forestry: 86.6%→97.7% (+11.1pp). Forest Rights: 67.4%→95.0% (+27.6pp). Conservation Principles: 67.2%→97.8% (+30.6pp). Tree Species: 82.6%→97.5% (+14.9pp).
C. MANAGEMENT & CONSERVATION

Management & Conservation: Intermediate Knowledge

Topics covering forest management planning, sustainable harvesting, fire prevention, reforestation methods, watershed protection, carbon sequestration, and wildlife corridors. Strong improvements observed across all topics, with Carbon Sequestration and Wildlife Corridors reaching 99.2% post-test scores.

Management & Conservation — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Performance (%)
Management Plans: 68.9%→83.4% (+14.5pp). Sustainable Harvesting: 69.6%→95.7% (+26.1pp). Fire Prevention: 85.2%→97.0% (+11.8pp). Reforestation: 67.0%→92.0% (+25.0pp). Watershed Protection: 76.4%→98.2% (+21.8pp). Carbon Sequestration: 90.8%→99.2% (+8.4pp). Wildlife Corridors: 88.6%→99.2% (+10.6pp).
D. POLICY & SUSTAINABILITY

Policy & Sustainability: Advanced Knowledge

Topics covering forest policy frameworks, climate adaptation, community governance, benefit sharing, ecosystem services valuation, agroforestry integration, and monitoring & evaluation. Strongest results with 6 of 7 topics exceeding 96% post-test, and Ecosystem Services achieving the highest score at 99.7%.

Policy & Sustainability — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Performance (%)
Forest Policy: 65.6%→95.3% (+29.7pp). Climate Adaptation: 74.7%→96.6% (+21.9pp). Community Governance: 96.9%→99.6% (+2.7pp). Benefit Sharing: 89.6%→98.6% (+9.0pp). Ecosystem Services: 88.5%→99.7% (+11.2pp). Agroforestry: 77.7%→97.9% (+20.2pp). Monitoring & Evaluation: 76.7%→97.4% (+20.7pp).
ADEQUATE KNOWLEDGE — BY LOCATION & GENDER

Knowledge Assessment Summary — Mangrove Restoration Training

Overall adequate knowledge improved dramatically from 36.9% to 81.5% (+44.6pp). By location, Kilifi showed the strongest gain (+59.0pp, from 21.1% to 80.1%), while Kwale improved from 53.7% to 83.1% (+29.4pp). By gender, females outperformed males at post-test (87.4% vs 80.2%), with females gaining +52.7pp compared to males' +42.0pp — demonstrating particularly strong knowledge uptake among women participants.

Adequate Knowledge by Location — Pre-Test vs Post-Test (%)
Kilifi: 21.1%→80.1% (+59.0pp — strongest location gain). Kwale: 53.7%→83.1% (+29.4pp). Overall: 36.9%→81.5% (+44.6pp). Kilifi closed the gap with Kwale significantly.
Adequate Knowledge by Gender — Pre-Test vs Post-Test (%)
Male: 38.2%→80.2% (+42.0pp). Female: 34.7%→87.4% (+52.7pp — strongest gender gain). Overall: 36.9%→81.9% (+45.0pp). Women outperformed men at post-test by 7.2pp.

49.3%

Status
BELOW TARGET
SEAWEED FARMING — KNOWLEDGE TEST RESULTS (BASELINE vs ENDLINE)

Knowledge Assessment Summary — Seaweed Farming Training

Overall adequate knowledge (≥70 points) declined from 67.4% at baseline to 29.3% at endline (−38.1pp). By gender, females dropped from 67.4% to 28.8% and males from 67.6% to 32.4%. At the ≥60 threshold, adequate knowledge went from 85.1% to 66.5%. Average standardized scores fell from 72.7 to 64.7, with females scoring 64.6 and males 65.2 at endline. Several questions saw large declines, with Q2 (−47.6pp), Q20 (−34.0pp), and Q5 (−27.5pp) being the most affected. However, Q8 (+30.0pp) and Q14 (+5.8pp) showed notable improvement.

Adequate Knowledge (≥70)
29.3%
↓ from 67.4% (−38.1pp)
Adequate Knowledge (≥60)
66.5%
↓ from 85.1% (−18.6pp)
Avg Score (All)
64.7
↓ from 72.7 (−8.0)
Avg Score (Female)
64.6
↓ from 72.8 (−8.2)
Avg Score (Male)
65.2
↓ from 72.6 (−7.4)
Adequate Knowledge by Threshold — Baseline vs Endline (%)
≥70 threshold: 67.4%→29.3% (−38.1pp). ≥60 threshold: 85.1%→66.5% (−18.6pp). Both thresholds show significant decline.
Adequate Knowledge (≥70) by Gender — Baseline vs Endline (%)
Female: 67.4%→28.8% (−38.6pp). Male: 67.6%→32.4% (−35.2pp). Both genders declined significantly.
Average Standardized Score by Gender — Baseline vs Endline
Female: 72.8→64.6 (−8.2). Male: 72.6→65.2 (−7.4). Overall: 72.7→64.7 (−8.0). Males slightly higher at endline.
Adequate Knowledge (≥60) by Gender — Baseline vs Endline (%)
Female: 85.5%→65.1% (−20.4pp). Male: 83.8%→76.5% (−7.3pp). Males retained more knowledge at ≥60 threshold.

Question-by-Question Analysis — Baseline vs Endline (% Correct)

# Baseline Endline Diff (E-B)
Q1. Three broad seaweed groups92.6%70.7%−21.8%
Q2. Seaweed group grown in Kenya52.6%4.9%−47.6%

Q4. Potential uses for seaweed50.9%27.4%−23.5%
Q5. Recommended farm has 10 plots74.3%46.8%−27.5%
Q6. Common seaweed farming methods7.4%3.4%−4.0%
Q7. Off-bottom method inputs96.6%81.4%−15.2%
Q8. Healthy seaweed characteristics28.6%58.6%+30.0%
Q9. Primary method to attach seaweed seedlings95.4%97.3%+1.9%
Q10. Statements regarding the process78.3%70.7%−7.6%
Q11. Routine farm management72.0%64.6%−7.4%

Q13. Disease affects seaweed96.6%97.0%+0.4%

Q16. Dried seaweed on ground74.9%58.6%−16.3%

Q18. Primary method to attach seaweed94.3%99.2%+5.0%
Q19. Common pest that affects seaweed65.7%69.6%+3.9%
Q20. Seaweed helps in reducing ocean acidification88.0%54.0%−34.0%
Q21. Most common harvesting method97.1%99.6%+2.5%
Q22. Seaweed should not be used as a biofertilizer46.9%52.1%+5.2%
Top Gains — Questions with Largest Improvement
Q8 Healthy seaweed characteristics (+30.0pp) was the standout improvement. Q14 Methods to control (+5.8pp) and Q22 Biofertilizer (+5.2pp) also improved.
Largest Declines — Questions with Biggest Drop
Q2 Seaweed grown in Kenya (−47.6pp) had the steepest decline. Q20 Ocean acidification (−34.0pp) and Q5 Farm plots (−27.5pp) also dropped sharply.
All 22 Questions — Baseline vs Endline (% Correct)
Visual comparison across all 22 knowledge questions. Green bars above baseline indicate improvement; red bars below indicate decline.

Indicator 1310c Performance

The percentage of targeted schools with access to sustainable clean water solutions exceeded the 60% target, rising from 23.4% at baseline to 62.5% at midline — a gain of +39.1pp. This represents a significant achievement, demonstrating that COSME interventions in water infrastructure, rainwater harvesting, and school-based water management are delivering measurable impact.

Indicator Baseline Midline Target Change Status
OUTPUT LEVEL — Schools Programme (1310-series)
1310c. Percentage of targeted schools with access to sustainable clean water solutions 23.4% 62.5% 60% +39.1pp EXCEEDED

A. Access to Clean Water: Baseline vs. Midline Comparison

Between the Baseline (September 2023) and Midline (July 2025) assessments

Borehole Access
52.3%
↑ from 44.2%
Rainwater Harvesting
27.7%
↑ from 13.0%
Water Tanks
9.2%
↑ from 2.6%
Piped Water Access
35.4%
↑ from 31.2%
Year-round Availability
55.4%
↑ from 49.4%
Indicator 1310c (Composite)
62.5%
↑ from 23.4%

Narrative Summary

Between the Baseline (September 2023) and Midline (July 2025) assessments, several indicators related to access to clean water in schools showed notable changes:

Water Source Improvements:

  • Borehole access increased from 44.2% to 52.3%
  • Rainwater harvesting rose from 13.0% to 27.7%
  • Water tanks usage increased significantly from 2.6% to 9.2%
  • Piped water access improved slightly from 31.2% to 35.4%
  • River and pond usage decreased, indicating a shift away from unsafe sources

Ownership of Water Infrastructure:

  • Schools with private ownership of water infrastructure increased from 63.6% to 81.5%
  • Public/communal ownership dropped from 36.4% to 15.4%

Additional Indicators:

  • Schools reporting year-round availability rose from 49.4% to 55.4%
  • Schools reporting clean/safe drinking water slightly decreased from 81.2% to 78.5%
  • Functional rainwater harvesting systems increased from 37.7% to 53.8%
  • The percentage of schools with sustainable clean water solutions rose from 23.4% to 62.5% (Indicator 1310c)
Water Source: Baseline vs Midline (%)
Infrastructure Ownership: Baseline vs Midline (%)
Key Indicators: Baseline vs Midline (%)
Indicator 1310c — Sustainable Clean Water Solutions (%)

General Insights & Trends

  • Clear trend toward improved infrastructure and safer water sources, particularly through increased borehole and rainwater harvesting systems
  • Shift from public to private ownership suggests greater school-level control and possibly better maintenance
  • Despite infrastructure gains, the perception of water safety declined slightly, which may reflect increased awareness or persistent quality issues

Recommendations

  • Strengthen water quality monitoring to address concerns about safety
  • Continue investing in rainwater harvesting and borehole development, especially in areas with limited piped water
  • Provide technical support and training for maintenance of water systems to ensure long-term sustainability
  • Investigate reasons behind the decline in perceived water safety despite infrastructure improvements

B. Water in Schools: Midline Results

Midline data from sections A, B, and C

Schools Received Tanks & Gutters
83%
Tanks Installed
34%
Low uptake
Gutters Installed
32%
Low uptake
Solvatten Kits Used
49%
14% partially used
Maintenance Budget
20%
46% partially
Hygiene Training Programs
74%

Narrative Summary

COSME Project Interventions:

  • 83% of schools received water tanks and gutters
  • However, only 34% of schools installed tanks, and 32% installed gutters
  • Usage rates were lower: 35% used tanks, 27% used gutters
  • Only 30% of schools had tanks and gutters connected for rainwater harvesting

Solvatten Kits:

  • All 65 schools received kits
  • 49% used them, 14% partially used, and 37% did not use them

Sustainability Measures:

  • 20% of schools had budgets for maintenance; 46% partially
  • 57% had trained staff/students managing systems
  • 65% conducted repairs/maintenance in the past year
  • 74% had hygiene training programs for students
  • 74% involved community members in oversight
  • 63% had income-generating activities linked to water use
Infrastructure: Received vs Installed vs Used (%)
Solvatten Kit Usage (%)
Sustainability Measures (%)
Tank & Gutter Pipeline: Received → Installed → Used (%)

General Insights & Patterns

  • While distribution of infrastructure was high, installation and usage lagged, suggesting barriers beyond access — likely financial or technical
  • Solvatten kits had better uptake than tanks/gutters, possibly due to ease of use
  • Schools are increasingly engaging in sustainability practices, but funding remains a major constraint
  • Community involvement and student training are strong, which bodes well for long-term impact

Recommendations

  • Provide installation support and technical assistance post-distribution
  • Develop maintenance funding models, possibly through community partnerships or government support
  • Expand training programs for staff and students to improve system management
  • Monitor and evaluate usage of distributed resources to ensure effectiveness

C. 4K Clubs: Midline Results

4K Club Establishment, Training & Support, and Environmental Initiatives

Schools with 4K Clubs
97%
3% had two clubs
Total Members
5,065
2,917 girls · 2,148 boys
Materials Received
92%
6% partially
Curriculum Completed ~50%
66%
14% almost, 17% less
Environmental Initiatives
81.5%
of clubs initiated activities
Patrons Agree: Adequate Training
85%

Narrative Summary

4K Club Establishment:

  • 97% of schools had at least one club; 3% had two
  • Total members: 2,917 girls and 2,148 boys

Training & Support:

  • 85% agreed patrons received adequate training
  • 88% agreed patrons actively support clubs
  • 85% agreed training materials are adequate
  • 89% agreed club members are committed
  • 89% agreed clubs are functional and active

Material Distribution:

  • 92% of schools received materials; 6% partially
  • Beneficiaries: 2,153 girls, 1,632 boys

Curriculum Delivery:

  • 66% of schools completed around half
  • 14% almost completed, 17% less than half

Environmental Initiatives:

  • 81.5% of clubs initiated activities
  • Most common: Tree planting (38), Farming (32), Environmental conservation (9)
Training & Support Agreement (%)
4K Club Members: Gender Split
Environmental Initiatives by Type
Curriculum Delivery Progress

General Insights & Patterns

  • 4K Clubs are widely adopted and well-supported, with high levels of engagement
  • Girls outnumber boys in club participation and material benefit
  • Environmental initiatives are diverse and impactful, with strong emphasis on sustainability
  • Curriculum delivery is progressing but needs acceleration to reach full completion

Recommendations

  • Support full curriculum implementation through targeted follow-ups and resources
  • Encourage gender-balanced participation, especially for boys
  • Expand environmental initiative guidance to help clubs diversify and deepen impact
  • Continue investing in training and materials, which are clearly valued and effective

Overall Insights

Cross-cutting findings across Schools Programme components

Financial Limitations Dominate

The most recurring theme is lack of financial resources. Schools frequently cited "cash" or "lack of funding" as the primary barrier to installing or completing water systems. This includes costs for tanks, gutters, plinths, and repairs.

Infrastructure Gaps

Many schools lack basic infrastructure such as storage tanks, gutters, or functional roofs for rainwater harvesting. Even when materials are provided, installation is often delayed due to lack of funds or technical support.

Security and Vandalism

Several schools face insecurity issues, including theft and vandalism of water infrastructure. The absence of perimeter fencing exacerbates these problems.

Community Needs and Shared Use

In some cases, water systems are shared with the local community, creating strain on limited resources and complicating maintenance responsibilities.

Environmental and Logistical Challenges

Schools in dry regions or with unreliable water sources struggle more, even when infrastructure is present. This underscores the need for context-specific solutions.

Positive Outcomes

  • A few schools reported successful installations that improved irrigation and water availability. These successes often involved community or parental support, highlighting the importance of local engagement.
  • A few schools report successful irrigation and crop watering, often linked to community or parental support.
  • Some schools express gratitude for support received, especially when water tanks help mitigate unreliable piped water.

Environmental Initiatives Engagement

Widespread Engagement: A total of 65 schools reported environmental initiatives, indicating strong engagement from youth clubs in environmental action.

Dominant Themes:

  • Tree Planting is the most common initiative, often linked to environmental conservation and climate resilience
  • Farming, including vegetable gardens and crop cultivation (e.g., cassava, maize, sukumawiki), is a close second, suggesting a strong link between environmental clubs and food security or income generation

Emerging Practices:

  • Waste Management and Water Conservation are less frequently mentioned but represent growing areas of interest
  • A few schools are exploring hygiene education, irrigation, and poultry farming, showing potential for diversification

Note: Many responses were vague or used unique phrasing, making them hard to categorize. This suggests a need for clearer guidance or standardized reporting formats.

Key Challenges Identified

  • Financial Constraints: Many schools cite lack of funds as a barrier to installing, maintaining, or repairing water systems (tanks, gutters, plinths)
  • Security Issues: Insecurity, including theft and vandalism, is a recurring theme. Schools without perimeter fencing are particularly vulnerable
  • Environmental Factors: Dry regions and unreliable water sources exacerbate maintenance challenges
  • Infrastructure Gaps: Even when materials are provided, installation is delayed due to lack of technical expertise or incomplete infrastructure
  • Community Pressure: Shared use of water systems with the community increases strain and complicates maintenance responsibilities

Seaweed Group Assessment

Seaweed Production, Value Addition & Commercialization — July 2025
Groups Surveyed
19 Groups
Active Female Members
610 Women
Assessment Period
July 2025

Indicator 1120b: Percentage and Number of targeted women with adequate resources to undertake regenerative and sustainable seaweed production, value addition and commercialization (disaggregated by type of activity)

Groups Assessed
19 Groups
Baseline
0%
Target
75%
Midline Result
34%
Status
ON TRACK

Indicator 1100c Performance

Indicator 1100c tracks the number of seaweed groups undertaking regenerative and sustainable seaweed production, value addition, and commercialization. Of the 19 groups surveyed: 17 groups (89.5%) practise regenerative/sustainable production, 13 groups undertake value addition (mostly drying), and 9 groups (47.4%) are actively selling seaweed or seaweed products.

Indicator 1100c Component Count Source Notes
OUTPUT LEVEL — Seaweed Programme (1100-series)
Regenerative & sustainable seaweed production 17 Section C.2 89.5% of all groups
Seaweed production value addition 13 Section B.2 Mostly drying (63.2%)
Seaweed commercialization 9 Section E.1 47.4% currently selling
A. GROUP PROFILE

Profile Summary

The majority of groups (42.1%) were formed between 2020–2022, indicating recent mobilization. 94.7% of groups are women-led, and 100% have formal leadership structures. Most groups meet weekly (73.7%), showing strong organizational engagement. Active female members total 610 across 19 groups (avg. 32.1 per group), with 175 active male members (avg. 9.2 per group).

Groups by Year of Establishment
42.1% of groups formed in 2020–2022, with 15.8% from 2023 onwards — showing rapid recent uptake driven by the COSME project and climate change awareness.
Women-Led Groups
94.7% of groups are led by women, and 100% have a formal leadership structure — demonstrating strong gender representation and organizational capacity.
Meeting Frequency
73.7% meet weekly indicating high engagement levels. 26.3% meet monthly. No groups report irregular meetings.
Active Members — Female vs Male (Summary Stats)
610 active female members (avg. 32.1/group) vs 175 active male members (avg. 9.2/group). Groups are predominantly driven by women, reflecting the project's focus on female empowerment.
B. INVOLVEMENT IN SEAWEED ACTIVITIES

Activities Summary

89.5% of groups are actively involved in seaweed-related activities. Production (89.5%) and drying (63.2%) are the most common, while value addition (26.3%) and marketing (10.5%) are less prevalent. 63.2% began seaweed activities from 2023 onwards, showing rapid uptake. Market opportunity (47.4%) and peer learning (36.8%) are the key motivators.

Type of Seaweed-Related Activity Undertaken
Production (89.5%) and drying (63.2%) dominate. Value addition (26.3%) and marketing (10.5%) remain underdeveloped — a key gap for program intervention.
Year Started Seaweed Activities
63.2% began from 2023 onwards, reflecting the COSME project's catalytic role. A significant number of these groups were recently mobilized, especially women-led groups.
Motivation to Engage in Seaweed Activities
Market opportunity (47.4%) is the top driver, followed by peer learning (36.8%). Project support accounts for 15.8%, while government training shows zero uptake.
Number of Ropes Owned by Groups
Total 27,689 ropes across 19 groups. 26.3% own 2,000+, while 15.8% have fewer than 100 — wide variation in production capacity. Average is 1,457 ropes per group.
C. SEAWEED PRODUCTION & SUSTAINABILITY PRACTICES

Production & Sustainability Summary

89.5% apply regenerative or sustainable practices, mostly trained by the COSME project (100%). Off-bottom method dominates (78.9%). However, 94.7% face challenges — mainly environmental stresses, resource constraints, and technical capacity issues. 78.9% report improved yield or quality after adopting sustainable practices. Production levels vary widely, with total harvest of 96,086 kg across groups.

Seaweed Production Methods Used
Off-bottom method dominates (78.9%), followed by double loop (15.8%) and long lines (10.5%). COSME project has standardized training around proven techniques.
Sustainable Practices Adoption & Training Source
89.5% apply regenerative practices, all trained by the COSME project. No other training provider — demonstrating the project's central role in sustainability adoption.
Challenges in Applying Sustainable Practices
Resource constraints (~30 mentions) and environmental challenges (~25) are the top barriers. Technical capacity issues (~15), health & safety (~10), and mobility (~10) also significant.
Changes in Yield/Quality After Adoption
78.9% report improved yield or quality after adopting sustainable practices. Only 15.8% saw no change, and 5.3% are unsure.
Seaweed Produced in Last Harvest (kg)
36.8% report zero production in last harvest. Total output: 96,086 kg, average 5,057 kg/group. One group produced 31,000 kg — far above the average of ~4,000 kg.
Production Records Keeping
63.2% keep harvest logs. 36.8% do not — a gap that undermines accurate production tracking and market negotiation capabilities.
D. ACCESS TO INPUTS AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT

Inputs & Support Summary

All groups received ropes and safety kits (100%), and 89.5% received seedlings. However, only 15.8% found inputs sufficient — most cite quantity and distribution issues. All received seaweed farming training, but fewer received business (26.3%), marketing (21.1%), or quality control (26.3%) support. Only 31.6% received business development assistance.

Inputs Received in Past 12 Months (by Type)
100% received ropes and safety kits. 89.5% received seedlings. Only 5.3% received drying racks, and no groups received boats — critical infrastructure gap.
Input Adequacy Assessment
Only 15.8% found inputs sufficient. 84.2% cite insufficiency due to quantity issues (~20), distribution problems (~10), quality issues (~8), and timing issues (~5).
Technical Training Received (by Type)
100% received seaweed farming training. Business (26.3%), quality control (26.3%), and marketing (21.1%) training reach fewer groups — a critical capacity gap.
Additional Support Needs Identified
Tools & equipment (~35 mentions) and training (~30) are the top needs. Infrastructure (~25) and market access (~20) follow. Financial support (~10) is less frequently cited.
E. COMMERCIALIZATION AND MARKET ACCESS

Commercialization Summary

47.4% of groups are currently selling seaweed products, mostly dried seaweed (100% of sellers) and raw seaweed/soap (55.6% each). Sales volume ranges widely — one group sold up to 27,000 kg. Average price is KES 81.1/kg (range 30–300), with only 55.6% negotiating prices. Transport (90%), demand (80%), and pricing (60%) are the top commercialization challenges. Only 10% have branding strategies.

Groups Selling Seaweed Products
47.4% are currently selling. 52.6% are not yet commercialized — despite production engagement, market linkages remain a bottleneck in the value chain.
Products Sold by Type (% of Selling Groups)
Dried seaweed (100%) dominates. Soap (55.6%) and raw seaweed (55.6%) follow. Gel (44.4%), shampoo (33.3%), and packaged products (11.1%) show value addition potential.
Market Types Used
Local markets (66.7%) and cooperative sales (44.4%) dominate. No exports or formal contracts — mostly local and direct buyers. Market linkages remain limited.
Commercialization Challenges (% of Selling Groups)
Transport (90%), demand (80%), and pricing (60%) are major barriers. Packaging (40%) and certification (30%) also cited. Only 10% have branding strategies.
Seaweed Price Distribution (KES/kg)
Most receive KES 50/kg (66.7%). Range: 30–300 KES/kg. Average: KES 81.1/kg. Only 55.6% negotiate prices — the rest accept buyer-fixed rates.
Impact on Group Income & Price Negotiation
70% report improved income/savings from commercialization. 55.6% negotiate prices, 44.4% accept buyer-fixed rates. Negotiation capacity is low but improving.
F. PARTNERSHIPS, SUSTAINABILITY & FUTURE PLANS

Partnerships & Future Plans Summary

NGOs and government are the key partners (63.2% each). Only one group has a formal agreement with buyers (5.3%). Future plans focus on value addition (~30 mentions), commercial expansion (~25), and training (~20). Critical needs for scaling include tools & equipment (~35), training & knowledge (~30), infrastructure (~25), and market access (~20).

Partners Involved in Seaweed Production
NGOs and government each support 63.2% of groups. Cooperatives (31.6%) are growing. Private sector (5.3%) and formal agreements (5.3%) remain negligible.
Future Plans in Seaweed Value Chain
Value addition (~30) and commercial expansion (~25) are top priorities. Training, infrastructure, and youth/women empowerment also featured prominently in group aspirations.
Critical Needs to Scale Activities
Tools & equipment (~35) and training (~30) are the most cited. Infrastructure (~25) and market access (~20) follow. Financial support (~10) is less frequently mentioned.
Formal Agreements with Buyers
Only 5.3% (1 group) have formal agreements — a critical gap. 94.7% lack formalized market relationships, leaving them vulnerable to price fluctuations and unreliable demand.
SUMMARY, INSIGHTS & RECOMMENDATIONS

A. Summary

The assessment of 19 seaweed groups in rural Kwale reveals a vibrant and rapidly growing movement, largely driven by women. Nearly all groups are women-led and meet regularly, with most convening weekly, indicating strong cohesion and organizational discipline. A significant number of these groups were established in the last five years, and the majority began seaweed-related activities only recently, particularly from 2023 onwards. This points to the catalytic role of the climate change project in mobilizing communities around seaweed farming.

Seaweed production is the most common activity, followed by drying and, to a lesser extent, value addition and marketing. While 89% of groups are actively engaged in seaweed work, only 47% are currently selling seaweed or its products. The most commonly sold items are dried seaweed and soap, with some groups venturing into gels and shampoos. However, commercialization remains limited, with most sales occurring in local markets, and only one group reporting a formal agreement with buyers.

Sustainability practices are widely adopted, with 89% of groups applying regenerative techniques such as off-bottom and double loop techniques. All training on these practices has been provided by the COSME project, and nearly all groups report challenges in implementation. These include environmental threats like strong waves and pests to logistical issues such as lack of boats and distant farm sites.

Production levels vary widely, with some groups reporting zero harvests and others producing up to 31,000 kg. Record keeping is practised by about two-thirds of the groups, and most reported improved yields after adopting sustainable practices. However, input adequacy is a major concern. While all groups received ropes and safety kits, only 15.8% feel the inputs were sufficient.

Training has focused heavily on technical farming skills, but support in business development, marketing, and quality control is far less common. This gap is reflected in the limited commercialization outcomes. Pricing is often fixed by buyers, and only half of the groups negotiate rates. Branding and packaging strategies are rare, and certification is virtually non-existent.

Looking ahead, groups express strong ambitions to expand their operations. Their future plans include scaling up production, engaging in value addition, building infrastructure, and empowering women and youth. To achieve these goals, they identify critical needs: better tools and equipment, more training, improved infrastructure, market access, and financial support.

Key Insights

  • High engagement, low adequacy: While nearly all respondents received some form of support, only a minority found it sufficient. This suggests a gap between provision and actual needs.
  • Strong agency gains: The high levels of reported confidence in decision-making and public speaking point to meaningful empowerment outcomes, even in the face of resource constraints.
  • Economic diversification: Investments in education, livestock, and small businesses indicate that seaweed farming is a catalyst for broader economic activity.
  • Environmental and social duality: Many women are involved in mangrove restoration and eco-tourism alongside seaweed farming, aligning with nature-based solutions. Yet, social resistance and health risks persist.
  • Outliers: One group sold 27,000 kg of seaweed — far above the average (~4,000 kg). Only one group has a formal agreement with buyers. Some groups report zero production despite having ropes and farm sites.
  • Record keeping and financial literacy: While 63% keep records and most received technical training, this hasn't translated into widespread savings or reinvestment.

Notable Patterns

  • Rapid Growth: Most groups started seaweed activities recently (2023+), showing strong program uptake.
  • Women's Leadership: Near-universal women-led structures suggest strong gender inclusion.
  • Sustainability Adoption: High uptake of regenerative practices, but challenges persist.
  • Training Gaps: While technical training is widespread, business and marketing support is limited.
  • Commercialization Bottleneck: Despite production, less than half are selling, and few have formal market linkages.

Recommendations for Program Improvement

1. Strengthen Market Linkages

  • Facilitate formal contracts with buyers and cooperatives
  • Support branding, packaging, and certification to access higher-value markets

2. Expand Business & Marketing Training

  • Include modules on pricing strategies, negotiation, and entrepreneurship
  • Promote peer-to-peer learning and exchange visits with successful groups

3. Improve Input Distribution

  • Ensure equitable and sufficient distribution of tools and materials
  • Introduce feedback mechanisms to monitor input adequacy and timing

4. Invest in Infrastructure

  • Prioritize drying sheds, storage facilities, and transport solutions
  • Explore community-owned boats or shared logistics to reduce post-harvest losses

5. Enhance Financial Access

  • Link groups to microfinance, VSLA schemes, or grant opportunities
  • Provide seed capital for value addition and commercialization

6. Deepen Sustainability Support

  • Address technical gaps with more localized and practical training
  • Support site feasibility studies and adaptive farming techniques

SEAWEED FARMERS PROFILING & CATEGORIZATION

Farmers are categorized by Ropes in Ocean into 5 tiers. Each tier has a tailored support strategy to maximize productivity and sustainable scaling.

⭐ Large Scale
300+ ropes in ocean
Support: Confidence to support
Support equals requested (meets ≥350 ropes target)
⭐ Upper Medium
200–299 ropes in ocean
Support: Given as requested
Meets target of ≥350 ropes
⭐ Lower Medium
100–199 ropes in ocean
Support: To reach 350 ropes
Focus on moving home ropes to ocean
⭐ Upper Small
50–99 ropes in ocean
Support: To reach 150 ropes
Focus on moving home ropes to ocean
⭐ Lower Small
<50 ropes in ocean
Priority: Move from home to ocean
Limited support to reach 50 ropes

🌡 Temperature Log Dashboard — Seaweed Drying Tables

Temperature monitoring across 8 bays at Funzi & Shangani | Feb 7–18, 2026 | 3 daily readings (06:00, 12:00, 18:00)

Total Readings
39
with temperature data

2
Funzi & Shangani

11 days

Avg Temperature
31.0°C
across all bays
Temp Range
18 – 47°C
min – max recorded
Daily Average Temperature by Location
Temperature by Time of Day
Average Temperature by Bay
Weather Impact on Temperature
Back Configuration Impact
Roller Position Impact
☀️ Midday Peak

Midday (12:00) temperatures average ~39°C across both locations — significantly higher than morning (~25°C) and evening (~30°C). Drying efficiency is highest at midday.

⛅ Weather Effect

Sunny conditions produce 9.3°C higher average temperature (36.0°C) compared to cloudy weather (26.7°C). Weather is a major driver of drying table performance.

🛠️ Configuration

Closed back configuration shows marginally higher temps (31.6°C) vs half-open (30.3°C). Roller-down position averages 1.8°C warmer than roller-up (31.6 vs 29.8°C).

% of VSLA Loans by Purpose

Loan Use Heatmap by County

Mangrove Resources Survey

Percentage of Mangrove Restoration Group Members with Adequate Resources
Average Score
50.6%
≥60 Threshold
54.3% (F) / 47.6% (M)
Gender Breakdown
Female & Male

Indicator 1110b: Percentage and Number of women and men with adequate resources to undertake mangrove restoration and conservation (disaggregated by sex)

Avg Score
50.6%
F: 51.3 | M: 48.6
Baseline
0%
Target
70%
Midline (≥60)
52%
F: 54.3% | M: 47.6%
Status
ON TRACK
Resource Adequacy — Threshold Analysis

Threshold Performance Summary

Members were scored on resource adequacy across Training, Seedlings, Materials, Sites, Financial Resources, Human Resources, and Overall perceptions. Scores at the ≥60 threshold show 54.3% female vs 47.6% male (52.0% overall). At the ≥40 threshold, 81.8% female vs 83.5% male (82.1% overall), indicating most members meet a basic adequacy level. The average score is 50.6 (Female: 51.3, Male: 48.6).

Threshold Attainment by Gender
At the ≥60 threshold, women outperform men (54.3% vs 47.6%). Gap narrows at ≥40 where men slightly lead (83.5% vs 81.8%).
Average Scores by Gender
Average resource adequacy score: Female 51.3, Male 48.6, Overall 50.6 — women consistently score higher across domains.
A. TRAINING

Training Summary

27.5% of members report training opportunities are "always available" (Female 27.5%, Male 36.2%, All 27.5%). Sufficiency of knowledge is rated "mostly sufficient" by 65.6% (Female 65.6%, Male 32.4%, All 60.8%). The training received is perceived as "very good" by 32.6% (Female 32.6%, Male 33.0%, All 32.6%) and "good" by 48.0%.

Availability of Training Opportunities (Past 12 Months)
Training is always available for 27.5% of all members; 36.2% of men vs 27.5% of women report consistent availability.
Sufficiency of Knowledge for Mangrove Restoration
65.6% of women feel mostly sufficient in their knowledge, but only 32.4% of men — a significant gender gap in perceived competence.
Perceived Quality of Training Received
80.6% rate training as very good or good overall. Minimal negative perception with only 1.2% rating it poor or very poor.
B. SEEDLINGS

Seedlings Summary

Seedlings/propagules are "always available" for only 19.9% of members (Female 19.4%, Male 21.4%). 56.1% report quantities are "mostly sufficient" for planned restoration. Quality and suitability are rated "good" by 50.0% and "very good" by 26.3%. However, 2.9% rate quality as poor, indicating some supply chain issues.

Availability of Mangrove Seedlings/Propagules
Only 19.9% say seedlings are always available. 35.3% say sometimes — supply is inconsistent for most groups.
Quantity Sufficiency for Planned Restoration
56.1% report mostly sufficient quantities. No members report completely insufficient — a positive sign for seedling supply chains.
Quality & Suitability of Seedlings (Species, Health, Survival)
76.3% rate quality as very good or good. Adequate at 4.6% and poor at 2.9% — quality is generally high but a few groups have concerns.
C. MATERIALS

Materials Summary

Only 12.7% rate planting materials supply as "very reliable" and 30.4% as reliable. Tools and equipment are "not available at all" for 42.1% of members (Female 41.9%, Male 43.9%). Sufficiency of tools is rated "completely insufficient" by 35.2%. Condition of tools is "very poor (often broken/unusable)" for 40.9% — a critical gap in material resources.

Reliability of Planting Materials Supply Across Seasons
Only 12.7% find supply very reliable. 24.0% say unreliable and 7.0% highly unreliable — seasonal gaps remain a challenge.
Availability of Tools and Equipment
42.1% have no tools at all. Only 11.1% report mostly available. A critical resource gap for restoration work.
Sufficiency of Tools and Equipment
35.2% say tools are completely insufficient. Only 7.0% rate as mostly sufficient. Major investment needed in equipment.
Condition and Quality of Tools
40.9% of tools are very poor (often broken/unusable). Only 11.1% in good condition. Tool maintenance and replacement is urgently needed.
D. SITES

Sites Summary

Restoration sites are "easily accessible" for 24.0% and "mostly accessible" for 28.8%. Security of permission is "fully secure" for 36.4% (Female 36.4%, Male 45.2%). Access or permission issues are "sometimes" experienced by 24.6% and "rarely" by 31.3% — suggesting moderate institutional barriers.

Accessibility of Mangrove Restoration Sites
24.0% find sites easily accessible. 18.1% say moderately accessible and 4.1% not accessible — geographic barriers affect some groups.
Security of Permission or Right to Work on Sites
36.4% have fully secure permission. 36.0% somewhat secure. Only 3.3% report very insecure access, suggesting moderate institutional support.
Frequency of Access/Permission Issues
33.9% rarely experience issues and only 1.8% very often. However, 24.6% experience issues sometimes — room for policy improvement.
E. FINANCIAL RESOURCES

Financial Resources Summary

Financial resources (grants, stipends, group funds) are "not available at all" for 39.8% of members (Female 18.6%, Male 46.2%). Sufficiency is rated "barely sufficient" by 26.9% and "completely insufficient" by 19.3%. Reliability of financial support is rated "unreliable" by 46.5% and "highly unreliable" by 16.3% — the biggest resource gap identified.

Availability of Financial Resources for Restoration
39.8% have no financial resources at all. Only 12.3% mostly available. Massive funding gap particularly for male members (46.2% no access).
Sufficiency of Financial Resources
No members report fully sufficient funds. 26.9% barely sufficient. Financial resourcing is the weakest domain across the survey.
Reliability of Financial Support
46.5% rate financial support as unreliable and 16.3% as highly unreliable. Only 4.4% find it reliable — a critical finding for donor engagement.
F. HUMAN RESOURCES

Human Resources Summary

Effectiveness in organizing and coordinating restoration activities is rated "very effective" by 31.1% (Female 15.7%, Male 26.2%) and "mostly effective" by 34.3%. Time availability is "fully sufficient" by 27.1% (Female 23.3%, Male 23.8%) while 14.6% report "very limited time." Labour reliability is rated "very reliable" by 32.2% and "somewhat reliable" by 43.7%.

Effectiveness in Organizing Restoration Activities
65.4% rate effectiveness as very or mostly effective. Only 5.8% rate as ineffective or very ineffective — groups are well organized.
Availability of Time for Restoration Activities
27.1% have fully sufficient time. 14.6% have very limited time and 2.4% no time available — competing livelihood demands affect participation.
Reliability of Group Labour
32.2% very reliable, 43.7% somewhat reliable. Only 5.8% unreliable and 0% highly unreliable — strong collective action.
G. OVERALL

Overall Summary

Perceived strength of NGO/government support: 29.8% rate as "strong support" (Female 26.7%, Male 33.3%) and 36.0% as "moderate support." Adequacy of all resources for mangrove restoration: only 12.3% rate as "fully adequate" (Female 11.6%, Male 14.5%) and 21.6% as "mostly adequate." 11.1% rate resources as "completely inadequate" — highlighting the need for sustained investment.

Perceived Strength of NGO/Government Support
29.8% perceive strong support. 11.1% report no support at all. Support is perceived but not strongly felt by most members.
Adequacy of All Resources for Mangrove Restoration
Only 12.3% say fully adequate. 11.1% completely inadequate. The majority (76.6%) fall in between — sustained investment needed across all resource domains.
Cross-Dataset Synthesis
Result Area Domain Women Baseline Women Midline Men Baseline Men Midline Change (pp) Trend Status
Comparative Analysis
Women vs Men: Key Result Area Comparison
Gender Gap Analysis: Midline vs Baseline
Domain Performance Radar
Logframe Achievement Overview
Progress Tracking & Targets
Baseline to Midline: Top Gains & Gaps
NbS Participation: Women vs Men Support
Training Effectiveness: Pre & Post Scores
Theory of Change Evidence Pathways

COSME's three-pronged strategy seeks to improve climate-resilient, equitable economies in coastal Kenya with a special focus on empowering women. Evidence pathways below are organized under the project's three intermediate outcomes, with cross-outcome linkages and critical tensions identified from midline data.

Endline Projections & Scenario Analysis

Based on baseline-to-midline trajectories, projected endline values under three scenarios: maintained momentum, accelerated effort, and regression risk.

Key Indicator Endline Projections (3 Scenarios)
Methodology & Data Quality Notes
Theory of Change Framework

Evidence pathways are structured around COSME's three intermediate outcomes (1100: NbS Adoption, 1200: Women's Agency, 1300: Governance). Each pathway maps Input → Output → Outcome → Impact using baseline-to-midline data across 35 result areas and 5 training measures. Logframe indicator IDs are shown in brackets for traceability. PMF composite indices (Resilience Index 1000a, WGEI 1000b) provide overarching outcome measurement.

Cross-Outcome Validation

Cross-outcome connections are validated against multiple datasets: VSLA group names confirm financial-to-NbS linkages (e.g., seaweed-named and conservation-named VSLAs), seaweed assessment data confirms women's leadership in value chains, and schools data confirms intergenerational pathways. Connection strength is rated as Strong, Moderate, Enabling, or Emerging.

Endline Projection Model

Projections extrapolate baseline-to-midline trajectories under three scenarios: Maintained (50% of gains continue), Accelerated (80% with intensified programming), and Regression (20% loss if interventions weaken). Projections use strategic logframe indicators prioritized over arbitrary array ordering. All values are capped at 0-100%.

Data Sources & Coverage

Data is drawn from 12 sources: Women COSME Survey (baseline+midline), Men COSME Survey (baseline+midline), GJJ Women/Men Surveys, Forestry Pre/Post Tests, Schools Assessments (65 schools), Seaweed Farming Assessment (19 groups, 519 farmers), Mangrove Resources Survey (resource adequacy), VSLA Monitoring (211 assessments, 173 groups), and PMF Composite Indices (RI, WGEI). Coverage spans Kilifi & Kwale Counties, Kenya.

Limitations & Caveats

Cross-outcome connections show temporal correlation, not established causation. Sample sizes vary across surveys, and self-reported data may introduce social desirability bias. Endline projections assume linear trajectories and equal intervention intensity. Tensions highlight where ToC assumptions diverge from observed data and may require adaptive programming.

Tension Detection Method

Tensions are identified when: (1) indicator pairs show contradictory trends (e.g., decision power +38pp vs resource access +12.6pp), (2) gender perception gaps exceed 20pp (e.g., business support: women -20.7pp vs men +14.1pp), or (3) value chain bottlenecks block impact conversion (e.g., 84.2% insufficient inputs in seaweed). Each tension is tagged to its affected outcome for accountability.

GRAPP — Gender Responsive Adaptation & Preparedness Plans

Participatory Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment for Gender Responsive Climate Adaptation and Preparedness Plans in Kilifi & Kwale Counties. 13 deliverables covering 6 wards, 2 counties, completed November 2025.

GRAPP Deliverables Registry

Deliverables by Type
Distribution of 13 GRAPP deliverables across document types: Ward Adaptation Plans (6), County Policy Briefs (2), County Climate Change Adaptation Action Plans (2), County Community Risk Preparedness Plans (2), and Funding Mechanisms Mapping (1).
Deliverables by County
Geographic distribution of deliverables between Kilifi County (3 wards: Mnarani, Mwarakaya, Sokoke) and Kwale County (3 wards: Pongwe-Kikoneni, Ramisi, Vanga), plus cross-county documents.
Climate Hazards Identified Across Wards
Key climate hazards identified across 6 wards through participatory climate risk and vulnerability assessments. Drought and flooding are the most prevalent hazards affecting all wards.
Vulnerability by Gender & Demographics
Gender-differentiated vulnerability analysis across wards showing women, youth, elderly, and persons abled differently (PAD) face distinct climate-related risks due to livelihood dependence, mobility, and resource access.
Priority Adaptation Actions by Sector
Proposed gender-responsive adaptation measures organized by sector: water & sanitation, agriculture & food security, ecosystem restoration, energy, livelihoods diversification, governance & institutional support, and early warning systems.
Ward-Level Climate Hazard Severity Matrix
Comparative assessment of climate hazard severity (High/Medium/Low) across the 6 target wards, based on participatory community assessments and environmental data.
Climate Funding Mechanisms & Opportunities
Mapping of available climate funding mechanisms at global (GCF, GEF, AF, CIFs), national (NCCF, NETFUND, KCIC), and county (CCCF, FLLoCA) levels for Kilifi and Kwale Counties.
Ecosystem Health Status by Ward
Status of key coastal and marine ecosystems (mangroves, forests, seagrass, coral reefs) across the 6 target wards based on current assessments.

Key Recommendations

COSME PMF – Midline Survey Results

This tab presents COSME Project Performance Measurement Framework (PMF) midline results, comparing Baseline, Midline and Target values for key indicators, and highlighting progress with traffic-light status.

PMF Indicator Performance Table

Baseline vs Midline vs Target — All Indicators

Index & Perception Indicators — Radar
Comparing Baseline, Midline and Target for Resilience Index (1000a), WGEI (1000b), Gender Equality Perception by Women (1210c) and by Men (1230b).
1100a — Area Under Rehabilitation (Hectares)
Gender-responsive NbS with biodiversity benefits: 63 ha rehabilitated at Midline against 123.8 ha target (50.9% progress).
1110a — Mangrove Knowledge by Gender
Female participants showed the strongest gain (+52.7pp), exceeding male participants. All subgroups exceeded the 57% target.
1200b — Women's Unpaid Work Time (Hours/Day)
Unpaid work time decreased from 9.3 to 7.7 hours/day, exceeding the 8.3-hour target. Lower is better.
Achievement Against Target (%)
Horizontal bar showing % achievement for each indicator. Green ≥100%, amber 80–99%, red <80%.
