Engagement Summary

GJJ

Gender Justice Journey — Annual Work Plan Data Presentation

A. Life Skills & Psychosocial Empowerment

Gender Justice Journey — Personal Agency, Self-Esteem & Communication Capacity

Indicator 1210b: Percentage of targeted women with adequate personal skills and confidence to assert their rights and express their decisions

Baseline
32.7%
Target
55.0%
Midline Result
46.3%
Change
+13.6pp
Status
ON TRACK

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Life Skills & Psychosocial Empowerment — Baseline vs Midline (n=345)
Self-Esteem & Confidence — % Agreeing/Strongly Agreeing
Self-respect: 78.2%→97.8% (+19.6pp). All self-esteem measures improved 5.9–19.6pp. Foundation for other empowerment outcomes.
Aspirations & Goal-Oriented Thinking
Know how to achieve goals: 72.1%→96.9% (+24.8pp). Having specific goals: 81.2%→99.1%. Planning capacity dramatically strong.
Leadership Capacity — Community Influence
Leadership ability 59.1%→94.1% (+35pp). Bringing people together 60.9%→92.5% (+31.6pp). Women becoming community mobilizers.
Communication Skills — Comfort & Effectiveness
Family expression 75.8%→95.6%. Community expression 52.5%→87.2% (+34.7pp). Others understanding better 59.6%→90.0%.

Source: GJJ KAP Women Survey (Endline) — SELF Domain

Self-Esteem & Self-Perception — Strongly Agree % (Baseline vs Endline) (n=312)
Strengths & Qualities
33.5% → 52.6%
+19.1pp
Feelings Important
28.9% → 49.4%
+20.5pp
Equal to Opposite Gender
20.4% → 48.2%
+27.8pp
Voice Opinions
26.7% → 65.9%
+39.2pp
SELF Domain Overview — Strongly Agree % (Baseline vs Endline)
All SELF domain statements show significant positive shifts. Largest gain: "Voice my opinions" +39.2pp. Average increase: +26.7pp across all 4 statements.
SELF Domain — Full Likert Scale Comparison
Shift from lower agreement to Strongly Agree category across all statements indicates deep internalisation of positive self-perception.

Source: GJJ KAP Women Survey (Endline) — RELATIONAL Domain

Relationship Quality & Communication — Frequency Distribution (Baseline vs Endline)
RELATIONAL Indicators — Always / Frequently % (Baseline vs Endline)
Strongest gains in "Respects when I say no" (27.4%→54.0%, +26.6pp) and "Discuss sharing responsibility" (39.3%→62.7%, +23.4pp). "Conflict escalates" declined (15.6%→13.8%), a positive sign. All 7 relational indicators improved except conflict escalation, which dropped favourably.
RELATIONAL Statements — Strongly Agree % (Baseline vs Endline)
Both relational statements nearly doubled in Strongly Agree: 23.2%→46.1% and 23.6%→46.1%. Substantial relational empowerment gains.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Indicator 1210b ON TRACK at 46.3% against 55% target (+13.6pp from 32.7% baseline) — already at 84% of target achievement
  • Self-esteem measures improved 5.9–19.6pp across all 5 dimensions; self-respect surged from 78.2% to 97.8% (+19.6pp)
  • Leadership capacity jumped 59.1% → 94.1% (+35pp) — women now see themselves as community mobilizers and change agents
  • Bringing people together for community initiatives: 60.9% → 92.5% (+31.6pp); others seeking their advice: 54.8% → 92.2% (+37.4pp)
  • "Voice my opinions in community" showed largest SELF domain gain: 26.7% → 65.9% (+39.2pp) — a transformational shift
  • Goal-oriented thinking near universal at midline: know how to achieve goals 72.1% → 96.9%, have specific goals 81.2% → 99.1%
  • Communication with community members showed strongest domain-wide improvement: 52.5% → 87.2% (+34.7pp)
  • SELF Domain "Equal to opposite gender" more than doubled in Strongly Agree: 20.4% → 48.2% (+27.8pp)
  • Relational empowerment statements nearly doubled — "partner treats me as equal": 23.6% → 46.1%; "discuss things calmly": 23.2% → 46.1%
  • Consent respect in relationships improved: 45.8% → 52.3% (+6.5pp) — strongest relational frequency gain

Strategic Insights

  • Agency transformation: Life skills programming successfully shifted mindsets from victimhood to agency — women now see themselves as leaders (94.1%), goal-setters (99.1%), and community influencers (92.5%)
  • Deepening internalisation: The shift from Agree to Strongly Agree across SELF domain items (average +26.7pp) indicates beliefs are being deeply internalised, not just superficially adopted
  • Cross-survey convergence: Both the Women COSME Survey and GJJ KAP Endline independently confirm strong empowerment gains — self-esteem, leadership, and communication improvements appear in both instruments, strengthening causal attribution to the programme
  • Ripple effects beyond individual: Relational domain improvements (partner equality statements doubling) suggest programme impact extends from personal empowerment into household dynamics and relationship quality
  • Communication as gateway skill: The 34.7pp surge in community communication comfort (52.5% → 87.2%) appears to underpin gains in leadership and community mobilisation — women who can express themselves effectively become agents of change
  • Gender perception breakthrough: "I see myself as equal to opposite gender" jumped +27.8pp — the largest Strongly Agree shift, signalling a fundamental rethinking of gender identity and worth
  • Near-ceiling effects: Aspirations and goal-setting at 96.9–99.1% at midline create measurement ceiling risks; endline instruments may need adjusted scales or new stretch indicators to capture continued growth
  • Nuanced relational findings: While "Feeling respected by partner" (Always) dipped 43.1% → 39.1%, the shift toward "Often" (28.3% → 31.7%) may reflect more realistic and nuanced self-assessment rather than decline — a common pattern with increased self-awareness
  • Pathway to endline target: At current trajectory (+13.6pp over baseline-midline period), the 55% target is achievable; sustained GJJ programming and community reinforcement will be key to closing the remaining 8.7pp gap
  • Programme design validation: The convergent, multi-dimensional improvements across self-esteem, aspirations, leadership, communication, self-perception, and relational quality validate the integrated GJJ + COSME life skills approach as an effective empowerment model

B. Social Norms & Gender Attitudes

Gender Justice Journey — Shifting Norms Around Roles, Responsibilities & Decision-Making

Indicator 1210c: Degree/extent to which women are perceived as equal to men

Baseline (Women)
42.1/100
Target
55.0/100
Midline (Women)
64.7/100
Change
+22.6pts
Status
EXCEEDED
Baseline (Men)
41.2/100
Target (Men)
55.0/100
Midline (Men)
71.0/100
Change (Men)
+29.8pts
Status (Men)
EXCEEDED

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Percentage of women agreeing or strongly agreeing with social norms statements
Women — Agreement with Social Norms Statements (Baseline vs Midline)
All 10 harmful norm statements declined. "Only men drive boats" plummeted 64.9%→9.1% (−55.8pp). "Man's job to earn, woman to care for home" fell 48.2%→22.7% (−25.4pp). "Cook/clean = good wife" dropped 65.4%→40.3% (−25.1pp). "Embarrassing for men to do chores" fell 31.7%→10.6% (−21.1pp). Deep and consistent norm transformation.

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Percentage of women stating roles and responsibilities should be 'Joint, both men and women'
Women — Roles & Responsibilities Should Be Joint (Baseline vs Midline)
All 10 role categories show dramatic increases towards joint responsibility. Largest gain: Prepare & cook food +58.8pp (12.5%→71.3%). Fetch water +56.4pp (21.4%→77.8%). Average gain across all roles: +47.5pp.

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Percentage of women stating decision-making should be 'Joint, both men and women'
Women — Decision-Making Should Be Joint (Baseline vs Midline)
Joint decision-making increased across all 11 categories. Largest gain: Go to market +42.6pp (28.0%→70.6%). Routine household purchases +27.5pp (35.0%→62.5%). All categories now at 62.5% or above.

Source: Men COSME Survey (Midline)

Percentage of men agreeing or strongly agreeing with social norms statements
Men — Agreement with Social Norms Statements (Baseline vs Midline)
Harmful norms declining significantly: "Man's job to earn money" dropped -34.7pp (63.1%→28.4%). "Inappropriate dress = her fault" dropped -36.9pp (67.6%→30.7%). "Only men should drive boats" dropped -36.5pp (59.2%→22.7%). Progressive norms strengthened: "Women stronger = communities stronger" 76.1%→79.5%.

Source: Men COSME Survey (Midline)

Percentage of men stating roles and responsibilities should be 'Joint, both men and women'
Men — Roles & Responsibilities Should Be Joint (Baseline vs Midline)
Men's views on joint roles shifted dramatically. Clean/sweep house +59.6pp (17.7%→77.3%). Prepare & cook food +50.5pp (17.7%→68.2%). Fetch firewood +56.8pp (14.8%→71.6%). Care for children +46.2pp (33.3%→79.5%). Average gain: +49.3pp.

Source: Men COSME Survey (Midline)

Percentage of men stating decision-making should be 'Joint, both men and women'
Men — Decision-Making Should Be Joint (Baseline vs Midline)
Joint decision-making endorsed by over 70% of men in all 11 categories at midline. Household loans +21.4pp (75.2%→96.6%). Routine purchases +34.1pp (54.5%→88.6%). Invest money +19.5pp (78.2%→97.7%). Highest: Invest money borrowed/saved 97.7%.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Women — Joint roles: All 10 household role categories surged toward joint responsibility. Preparing food jumped 12.5% → 71.3% (+58.8pp); fetching water 21.4% → 77.8% (+56.4pp)
  • Women — Joint decisions: All 11 decision categories improved. "Go to market" rose 28.0% → 70.6% (+42.6pp). Financial decision-making (savings, loans, investments) now at 87–89%
  • Men — Harmful norms declining: "Man's job to earn money; woman to look after home" dropped 63.1% → 28.4% (−34.7pp). "Only men should drive boats" dropped 59.2% → 22.7% (−36.5pp)
  • Men — Joint roles: Cooking as joint duty surged 17.7% → 68.2% (+50.5pp). Cleaning house 17.7% → 77.3% (+59.6pp). All 10 categories now above 68%
  • Men — Joint decisions: "Invest money borrowed or saved" reached 97.7% at midline. "Household loans" reached 96.6%. All 11 categories now above 70%
  • Embarrassment about domestic chores (men) dropped from 30.4% → 11.4% (−19.0pp) — practical norm change in action
  • Progressive statement maintained: "Stronger women = stronger communities" held at 76.1% → 79.5% — foundational belief intact
  • "When women gain rights, men lose" declined from 28.4% → 13.6% (−14.8pp) — zero-sum thinking eroding

Strategic Insights

  • Convergent norms shift: Both men and women independently report dramatic increases in endorsing joint roles and joint decision-making — the shift is not one-sided but mutual and reinforcing
  • Domestic sphere breakthrough: Cooking, cleaning, and childcare — traditionally the most gendered domestic tasks — showed the largest gains (50–60pp), indicating deep norm change beyond surface attitudes
  • Financial empowerment convergence: Joint financial decision-making (savings, loans, investments) now exceeds 87% for both genders — financial partnerships are becoming the norm rather than the exception
  • Structural masculinity norms eroding: The dramatic drops in "man's job to earn money" (−34.7pp), "only men drive boats" (−36.5pp), and "embarrassing for men to do chores" (−19pp) indicate structural masculinity norms are actively being renegotiated
  • GBV-related attitudes improving: "Dressed inappropriately = her fault" halved from 67.6% to 30.7% (−36.9pp) — a critical shift for violence prevention. However, 30.7% remaining suggests continued programming needed
  • Remaining resistance area: "Men have better business ideas" only decreased from 41.9% to 38.6% (−3.3pp) and "Good wife cooks/cleans" increased 64.6%→69.3% — these represent sticky traditional norms that may need targeted intervention
  • Programme design validation: The parallel GJJ programming for both men and women is producing convergent results — men and women are shifting together toward more equitable attitudes, reducing the risk of backlash from one-sided empowerment
  • Scale of transformation: Average gains of 47–49pp across all role categories for both genders within one programme cycle represents unusually rapid norm change — suggesting strong programme design and community engagement

C. Climate Change & NbS Knowledge

Awareness of Climate Change, Nature-based Solutions & Ecosystem Restoration

Indicator 1210a: Percentage of targeted women with adequate practical knowledge on NbS, Economic Rights and GE&I

Baseline
20.7%
Target
55.0%
Midline Result
80.9%
Change
+60.2pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Climate Change Awareness & Effects Identification
Heard of Climate Change (Baseline)
60.5%
Not Heard (Baseline)
39.5%
Women — Effects of Climate Change on Environment (Baseline vs Midline)
Increased drought recognition rose 54.7%→75.9% (+21.2pp). Hotter temperatures 39.5%→67.5% (+28.0pp). "I do not know" dropped from 21.2% to 4.1% (−17.1pp). Understanding of ocean-related impacts improved but remains low.

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Nature-based Solutions (NbS) Awareness & Examples
Heard of NbS (Baseline)
23.1%
Heard of NbS (Midline)
77.8%
Change
+54.7pp
Women — Examples of NbS (Baseline vs Midline)
Reforestation recognition surged 13.6%→64.1% (+50.5pp). Mangrove restoration 10.1%→40.0% (+29.9pp). "I do not know" dropped 77.1%→22.8% (−54.3pp). Sustainable agriculture up 10.5%→19.4%.
Women — Awareness of NbS (Baseline vs Midline)
NbS awareness tripled from 23.1% to 77.8%. "Do not know" NbS plunged from 76.9% to 22.2% — a +54.7pp improvement in foundational NbS knowledge.

Source: Women COSME Survey (Midline)

Knowledge of Mangrove Restoration, Seaweed Farming & Forest Management
Women — Mangrove Restoration Awareness
Full understanding (restoration + importance): 20.9%→60.7% (+39.8pp). "Not heard" collapsed from 33.8% to 3.8%. Near-universal mangrove awareness at midline (96.2%).
Women — Seaweed Farming Awareness
Full understanding (farming + economic/ecosystem benefits): 15.2%→41.0% (+25.8pp). "Not heard" dropped from 66.0% to 39.9%. Seaweed awareness still has room for growth.
Women — Forest Management & Conservation
Forest management awareness reached near-universal: 65.7%→97.8% (+32.1pp). "Not heard" collapsed from 34.3% to just 2.2%. Forest-related programming showing strongest ecosystem knowledge gains.

Source: Men COSME Survey (Midline)

Climate Change Awareness & Effects Identification
Heard of CC (Baseline)
77.8%
Heard of CC (Midline)
100.0%
Change
+22.2pp
Men — Effects of Climate Change on Environment (Baseline vs Midline)
Increased drought recognition rose 66.9%→80.7% (+13.8pp). Hotter temperatures 53.3%→76.1% (+22.8pp). Extreme weather events 18.9%→37.5% (+18.6pp). "I do not know" nearly eliminated: 11.3%→1.1%.

Source: Men COSME Survey (Midline)

Nature-based Solutions (NbS) Awareness & Examples
Heard of NbS (Baseline)
42.4%
Heard of NbS (Midline)
89.8%
Change
+47.4pp
Men — Examples of NbS (Baseline vs Midline)
Reforestation recognition surged 57.1%→81.0% (+23.9pp). Mangrove restoration 43.6%→57.0% (+13.4pp). Sustainable agriculture 27.9%→39.2% (+11.3pp). "I do not know" eliminated: 1.8%→0.0%.

Source: Men COSME Survey (Midline)

Knowledge of Mangrove Restoration, Seaweed Farming & Forest Management
Men — Mangrove Restoration Awareness
Full understanding (restoration + importance): 27.6%→64.5% (+36.9pp). "Not heard" collapsed from 17.3% to 3.2%. Near-universal mangrove awareness (96.8%).
Men — Seaweed Farming Awareness
Full understanding (farming + economic/ecosystem benefits): 18.5%→43.5% (+25.0pp). "Not heard" dropped 51.1%→41.9%. Similar seaweed awareness trajectory as women.
Men — Forest Management & Conservation
Forest management awareness reached universal: 75.7%→100.0% (+24.3pp). All men now aware of forest management and conservation at midline.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Climate change awareness: Women baseline 60.5%, men baseline 77.8% rising to 100% at midline. Universal climate awareness achieved among men
  • NbS awareness — Women: Tripled from 23.1% → 77.8% (+54.7pp). "Do not know" cut from 76.9% to 22.2%
  • NbS awareness — Men: Rose from 42.4% → 89.8% (+47.4pp). "Do not know" NbS examples eliminated (1.8%→0.0%)
  • Top recognised effect: Increased drought — women 54.7%→75.9%; men 66.9%→80.7%. Hotter temperatures — women 39.5%→67.5%; men 53.3%→76.1%
  • Reforestation: Women surged 13.6%→64.1% (+50.5pp). Men: 57.1%→81.0%. Top NbS example for both genders
  • Mangrove restoration: Full understanding — women 20.9%→60.7% (+39.8pp); men 27.6%→64.5% (+36.9pp). "Not heard" collapsed to 3–4%
  • Forest management: Women 65.7%→97.8%; Men 75.7%→100.0%. Near-universal awareness achieved for both genders
  • Seaweed farming: Full understanding — women 15.2%→41.0% (+25.8pp); men 18.5%→43.5% (+25.0pp). "Not heard" still at ~40% — largest remaining awareness gap
  • "I do not know" eliminated: Climate effects "don't know" dropped to 4.1% (women) and 1.1% (men). NbS "don't know" dropped to 0% for men

Strategic Insights

  • Knowledge revolution: NbS awareness among women tripling (23.1%→77.8%) represents one of the most dramatic knowledge shifts in the programme — community-level environmental literacy is transforming rapidly
  • Gender knowledge gap closing: Baseline showed men consistently ahead of women in environmental knowledge; midline shows convergence — women narrowing the gap in NbS examples, climate effects, and restoration awareness
  • Terrestrial vs marine knowledge disparity: Forest management (97–100%) and reforestation (64–81%) vastly outpace seaweed farming (41–44%) and ocean-related impacts (1–23%). Programming may need to strengthen marine/coastal literacy modules
  • Experiential learning effect: The strongest knowledge gains align with visible, hands-on programme activities (mangrove planting, forest nurseries, reforestation) — suggesting experiential learning drives knowledge more effectively than classroom-only approaches
  • Scientific literacy gap: Ocean acidification (0.6–1.3%), species migration (2–5%), and vector-borne diseases (1–5%) remain poorly understood — more complex climate science concepts need simplified, visual communication strategies
  • Seaweed opportunity: With 40% of both genders still not having heard of sustainable seaweed farming, this represents the largest remaining awareness gap and a clear opportunity for an intensified seaweed module
  • Foundation for action: Near-universal awareness of climate change and NbS creates fertile ground for the programme's next phase — transitioning from knowledge to practice and sustained community-led conservation behaviours
  • Programme attribution strong: The parallel, large-scale improvements across both genders and multiple knowledge domains (NbS +47–55pp, reforestation +24–51pp, mangrove +37–40pp) provide strong evidence of programme impact rather than secular trends

D. Decision Making

Gender Justice Journey — Women's Participation in Household & Enterprise Decision-Making

Indicator 1200a: Percentage of targeted women with adequate involvement in HH, group and enterprise decision making

Baseline
57.6%
Target
65.0%
Midline Result
95.9%
Change
+38.3pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Women Experiencing 'Joint, Both Men and Women' Decision Making

Percentage of women stating decisions are made jointly in their household
Joint Decision Making in Household (Baseline vs Midline)
All 11 decision areas show significant increases toward joint decision-making. Largest gains: Routine mangrove/seaweed/forest nursery work +39.3pp (9.8%→49.1%), going to market +34.6pp (13.5%→48.1%), and routine HH purchases +25.0pp (25.6%→50.6%). Education of children had the smallest change (+1.6pp) but started highest (58.1%).

Women Who Can Influence HH Decisions 'To a Large Extent'

Percentage of women stating they can influence household decisions to a large extent
Women's Influence on HH Decisions — 'To a Large Extent' (Baseline vs Midline)
All 11 areas show improved influence. Mangrove/seaweed/forest nursery work saw the largest leap: +34.9pp (17.0%→51.9%). Investment decisions improved +16.6pp (39.7%→56.3%). HH savings influence rose +16.1pp (40.8%→56.9%). Even areas with already-moderate baseline levels grew, demonstrating broad-based empowerment.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Target massively exceeded: Indicator 1200a reached 95.9% against a 65% target (+38.3pp from baseline of 57.6%), indicating transformative change in women's participation in decision-making
  • Joint decision-making surged across all areas: Average increase of +22.3pp across all 11 decision categories, with no area declining
  • Previously excluded areas transformed:Mangrove/seaweed/forest nursery decisions — where women had minimal input (9.8%) — now show near-parity (49.1%), a +39.3pp gain
  • Economic decisions democratised: Joint decisions on market access (+34.6pp), routine purchases (+25.0pp), and large purchases (+24.3pp) reflect fundamental shifts in household economic governance
  • Women's influence deepened: The proportion of women who can influence decisions 'to a large extent' increased across all 11 categories, with an average gain of +14.0pp

Programme Insights

  • Dual-track progress validated: Both joint decision-making structure (who decides) and women's perceived influence (how much say) improved in parallel — suggesting genuine power shifts, not token participation
  • GJJ programming impact: The Gender Justice Journey approach of engaging both men and women is reflected in the shift from male-dominated to joint decision-making across traditionally male-controlled domains (loans, large purchases, market access)
  • Influence gap narrowing: The largest influence gains occurred in areas where women previously had least say — nursery work (+34.9pp), savings (+16.1pp), investments (+16.6pp) — indicating targeted empowerment in economic spheres
  • Education decisions already joint: Education of children showed the smallest change (+1.6pp joint, +9.1pp influence) because it was already the most jointly-decided area (58.1% baseline), suggesting it was a cultural starting point for shared governance
  • Scale of transformation: Moving from 57.6% to 95.9% adequate involvement in one programme cycle represents exceptional results — nearly universal participation in household decision-making among targeted women

E. Access & Control

Gender Justice Journey — Women's Ownership of Assets & Access to Productive Inputs

Indicator 1200c: Percentage of targeted women with adequate access and control over household and enterprise resources

Baseline
41.2%
Target
50.0%
Midline Result
53.8%
Change
+12.6pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Women Who Own Assets (Solely or Jointly)

Percentage of women reporting sole or joint ownership of household assets
Women's Asset Ownership — Solely or Jointly (Baseline vs Midline)
Fuel-efficient cooking stoves saw the largest gain: +54.3pp (11.6%→65.9%). Solar panels (+15.6pp), small livestock (+15.4pp), and non-mechanized farm equipment (+14.3pp) also rose significantly. Some assets declined — household furniture (−21.7pp), television (−12.1pp), house/building (−7.5pp) — possibly reflecting changes in survey interpretation or asset redistribution patterns.

Women With Access to Productive Inputs

Percentage of women stating inputs are available in their HH and they can use them
Women's Access to Productive Inputs (Baseline vs Midline)
Natural/organic fertilizers showed the largest gain: +17.8pp (32.2%→50.0%). Animal feed access tripled: +17.1pp (8.5%→25.6%). Veterinary drugs rose +9.8pp (16.1%→25.9%). Seeds declined slightly (−6.4pp) and aquaculture inputs (fingerlings, fish feed) remain very low or dropped to zero, reflecting the programme's land-based rather than aquaculture focus.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Target exceeded: Indicator 1200c reached 53.8% against a 50.0% target (+12.6pp from 41.2% baseline), showing meaningful progress in women's access to and control over resources
  • Energy access transformed: Fuel-efficient cooking stoves ownership surged from 11.6% to 65.9% (+54.3pp) — the single largest asset gain, likely linked to programme distribution or promotion activities
  • Productive asset gains: Solar panels (+15.6pp), small livestock (+15.4pp), non-mechanized farm tools (+14.3pp), and cellphones (+8.5pp) all increased, strengthening women's economic capacity
  • Agricultural input access improved: Natural fertilizers (+17.8pp), animal feed (+17.1pp), and veterinary drugs (+9.8pp) increased, aligning with NbS and livelihood programming
  • Mixed results in traditional assets: Furniture (−21.7pp), TV (−12.1pp), and house/building (−7.5pp) declined — may reflect economic pressures, asset redistribution, or survey methodology differences

Programme Insights

  • Programme-linked assets rising: The strongest gains are in assets directly linked to COSME programming — fuel-efficient stoves, solar panels, farm tools, and livestock — suggesting successful asset-building interventions
  • Input access aligns with NbS focus: The surge in organic fertilizers (+17.8pp) and animal feed (+17.1pp) connects directly to the nature-based solutions and sustainable agriculture training provided by the programme
  • Aquaculture gap persists: Fingerlings dropped to 0% and fish feed to 0.9% — indicating that aquaculture inputs remain outside the programme's current scope or supply chain reach
  • Digital inclusion progressing: Cellphone ownership rose from 74.9% to 83.4% (+8.5pp), supporting women's access to information, mobile money, and market connectivity
  • Need for deeper analysis: The decline in some traditional assets (furniture, TV, housing) warrants investigation — whether this reflects genuine asset loss, changes in joint ownership reporting, or sampling differences between baseline and midline

F. Time Poverty, Roles & Responsibilities

Gender Justice Journey — Women's Time Use, Unpaid Care Work & Household Task Distribution

Indicator 1200b: Average time women spend in unpaid work

Baseline
9.3 hours
Target
8.3 hours
Midline Result
7.7 hours
Change
-1.6 hrs
Status
EXCEEDED

Time Investment in Daily Activities (Hours)

Average hours women spend on different categories of daily work — reduction through programme
Time Investment in Daily Activities (Hours) — Reduction Through Program
Unpaid care work reduced 1.6 hours (17% decrease). Productive work increased 0.7 hours, positive reallocation. Still challenging — women averaging 12.6 hours of work daily.

Actual Experience: Women Performing These Tasks

Percentage of women reporting they personally perform key household tasks
Women Personally Performing Household Tasks (Baseline vs Midline)
Significant progress: Household task distribution improved dramatically from baseline. Baseline showed 83% performing firewood, 85% cleaning, 87% cooking. Midline reduced to 37%, 33%, 29% respectively — indicating successful norm shift toward joint responsibility. Continued engagement needed to sustain gains.

Joint Responsibilities: 'Both Men & Women' in the Household

Percentage of women experiencing joint (men and women) responsibility for household tasks
Women Experiencing 'Joint, Both Men and Women' Responsibilities in HH (Baseline vs Midline)
Joint responsibility increased across all 10 household tasks. The largest gains were in food preparation (+32.2pp), cleaning (+33.5pp), and childcare (+30.8pp) — traditionally female-dominated tasks. Income provision saw the highest absolute joint responsibility at midline (47.2%), up from 18.0%. Even the lowest-gaining task (firewood) showed a +26.8pp increase to 32.5%.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Target exceeded: Indicator 1200b dropped from 9.3 to 7.7 hours of unpaid work (target was 8.3 hours), a 17% reduction representing 1.6 fewer hours of daily unpaid labour for women
  • Dramatic task redistribution: Women solely performing cooking dropped from 87% to 29% (−58pp), cleaning 85%→33% (−53pp), firewood 83%→37% (−46pp), water fetching 77%→22% (−54pp), and childcare 71%→26% (−45pp)
  • Joint responsibility surged: Across all 10 tasks, joint responsibility increased by an average of +29pp, with food preparation (+32pp), cleaning (+34pp), and childcare (+31pp) showing the strongest shifts
  • Productive work increased: Time on productive work rose from 4.2 to 4.9 hours (+0.7 hrs), indicating women are redirecting freed-up time toward income-generating activities
  • New time categories emerged: Community work (0.4→1.8 hrs) and personal development (0→0.3 hrs) appeared at midline, suggesting women now have time for broader engagement beyond household duties

Programme Insights

  • Gender norm shift is real: The massive move from women-only to joint household responsibility (average +29pp across 10 tasks) shows that men are increasingly sharing domestic duties — a fundamental gender norms outcome
  • Unpaid care reduction works: The 1.6-hour daily reduction in unpaid care translates to ~584 additional free hours per woman per year — time that can be invested in livelihoods, education, and personal growth
  • Time reallocation is positive: The shift from unpaid care (−1.6 hrs) to productive work (+0.7 hrs) and community work (+1.4 hrs) shows women leveraging freed time for economic and social empowerment
  • Still a heavy total burden: Despite gains, women still average 12.6 hours of total daily work (paid + unpaid), highlighting the ongoing need for labour-saving technologies, infrastructure, and deeper norm change
  • Income provision most equitable: Being the main income provider/buying food reached the highest joint responsibility (47.2%), reflecting progress in economic partnership within households — a critical outcome for Women's Economic Empowerment

G. Family Support

Gender Justice Journey — Men's Support for Female Household Members in NbS Livelihood Activities

Indicator 1230a: Percentage of male family members with adequate knowledge and practical skills to promote and support NbS, economic rights of women and GE&I

Baseline
54.70%
Target
65.00%
Midline Result
80.3%
Change
+25.6pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Men Supporting Female HH Members — Overview

Percentage of men reporting 'Yes' support across three NbS livelihood activities
Men Supporting Female HH Members — Yes / No / Somehow (Baseline vs Midline)
Male support surged across all three activities. Mangrove support ('Yes') jumped from 29.7% to 80.7% (+51pp). Seaweed support leapt from 15.7% to 83.9% (+68.2pp). Forest support reached 95.8% at midline, up from 33.7% (+62.1pp). 'No' responses dropped dramatically — to 17.5%, 0%, and 0% respectively.

Type of Support — Mangrove Restoration

How men support female HH members in their mangrove restoration efforts
Men's Type of Support for Mangrove Restoration (Baseline vs Midline)
Active support forms all increased: direct mangrove work support doubled (13.1%→27.3%), encouragement more than doubled (8.9%→21.8%), and household chore support rose (8.9%→20.0%). 'None' dropped sharply from 61.4% to 21.8%. Community support-seeking tripled (1.2%→3.6%) though remains low.

Type of Support — Seaweed Farming

How men support female HH members in their seaweed farming efforts
Men's Type of Support for Seaweed Farming (Baseline vs Midline)
Seaweed farming work support saw the largest increase: 8.2%→28.0% (+19.8pp). Encouragement rose from 2.7% to 10.0%, and material/input purchase support increased (1.9%→6.0%). 'None' halved from 82.5% to 50.0%, though remains high — suggesting room for further engagement in this activity.

Type of Support — Forest Management & Conservation

How men support female HH members in their forest management and conservation efforts
Men's Type of Support for Forest Management & Conservation (Baseline vs Midline)
Encouragement saw the largest gain: 6.5%→41.7% (+35.2pp). Material/input purchase support surged (2.4%→25.0%). Community support-seeking rose from 0.8% to 4.2%. 'None' collapsed from 45.5% to just 4.2% — the strongest engagement shift across all three NbS activities. Direct forest work and household chore support saw slight reductions, possibly reflecting a shift toward other support types.

Summary, Highlights & Insights

Key Highlights

  • Target exceeded: Indicator 1230a reached 80.3% against a 65.0% target (+25.6pp from 54.7% baseline), demonstrating significant growth in men's knowledge and skills to support NbS, economic rights, and GE&I
  • Forest support near-universal: 95.8% of men now support female HH members in forest management (up from 33.7%), with 'None' collapsing from 45.5% to just 4.2% — the strongest shift across all three activities
  • Seaweed support transformed: Male support for seaweed farming jumped from 15.7% to 83.9% ('Yes'), the largest absolute gain (+68.2pp) among the three activities
  • Mangrove engagement strong: Support rose from 29.7% to 80.7%, with direct mangrove work support doubling (13.1%→27.3%) and encouragement more than doubling (8.9%→21.8%)
  • Disengagement plummeting: 'None' responses dropped across all activities — mangrove (61.4%→21.8%), seaweed (82.5%→50.0%), forest (45.5%→4.2%) — showing men actively moving from passive to supportive roles

Programme Insights

  • Men's engagement model works: The dramatic increase across all support categories demonstrates that the programme's male engagement strategy — combining awareness, skills-building, and norm change — is producing tangible behavioural shifts
  • Encouragement is the leading support type: Across activities, encouragement consistently grew the fastest (especially forest: +35.2pp), suggesting men are moving from indifference to active advocacy for women's participation
  • Seaweed support has room to grow: While 'Yes' support reached 83.9%, 'None' for seaweed support types remains at 50% — indicating men declare support but may not yet be providing specific tangible assistance in this activity
  • Forest as a model: Forest management shows the most complete transformation — near-universal support (95.8%), lowest 'None' (4.2%), and the widest range of active support types. This pattern can inform engagement strategies for mangrove and seaweed
  • Material support emerging: Purchase of materials/inputs for forest activities surged (2.4%→25.0%), showing men are investing financially — a deeper level of support beyond time and encouragement that strengthens women's economic participation

Resilience Building Assets

Savings, Loans & VSLA Performance — Women Survey Midline

A. Savings

Resilience Building Assets — Savings Behaviour, Mechanisms & Loan Access

Indicator 1220a: Percentage of targeted women and young women who report having savings

Baseline
28.8%
Target
70.0%
Midline Result
91.9%
Change
+63.1pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Women Personally Saving

Saving Mechanisms Used

Intended Use of Savings

Women Taking Loans

VSLA Summary — Data Monitoring (June 2025)

Metric Kilifi Kwale Total
VSLAs Assessed 58 82 140
Total Members 1,500+ 2,200+ 3,700+
Female Members 1,150 1,750 2,900
Male Members 350+ 450+ 800+
Members Left 224 338 562
Attrition Rate 8.3% 10.0% 9.3%
Actively Saving 1,037 1,723 2,760
Total Savings (KES) 10,155,460 12,688,614 22,844,074
Loans Disbursed (KES) 6,825,600 10,291,500 17,117,100
Loans Repaid (KES) 4,032,350 6,351,400 10,383,750
Interest Earned (KES) 910,040 1,439,410 2,349,450
Social Fund (KES) 1,363,010 1,818,270 3,181,280

Key Insights — Savings

  • Massive savings uptake: Women personally saving surged from 43.1% to 93.4% — a near-universal adoption driven by VSLA membership and structured savings mechanisms
  • VSLA dominance: VSLA became the overwhelmingly preferred saving mechanism (43.6%→98.0%), displacing informal methods. At-home saving dropped to 0.0% and bank saving fell to 2.7%
  • Asset-oriented saving: The biggest shift in intended use is toward large household assets (10.2%→63.9%), signalling a move from consumption-based saving to investment-oriented financial behaviour
  • Loan access transformed: Women taking loans nearly tripled (28.0%→79.1%), with VSLA providing accessible credit that formal institutions could not — supporting productive investments and household resilience
  • Strong VSLA performance: 140 VSLAs with 3,700+ members (2,900 female, 800+ male) have accumulated KES 22.8M in savings, disbursed KES 17.1M in loans, and maintained a 9.3% attrition rate — with 2,760 members actively saving across Kilifi (1,037) and Kwale (1,723)

B. Solar Panels & Cooking Stoves

Resilience Building Assets — Access to Time-Saving & Environmentally Friendly Technologies

Indicator 1220b: Percentage of women with access to time-saving and environmentally friendly solar panels and cooking stoves

Baseline
50.8%
Target
80.0%
Midline Result
83.1%
Change
+32.3pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Solar Panels — Ownership Pattern

Fuel-Efficient Cooking Stoves — Ownership Pattern

Baseline vs Midline — Combined Asset Access Comparison

Key Insights — Solar Panels & Cooking Stoves

  • Cooking stoves transformation: Fuel-efficient cooking stoves saw the most dramatic growth — household access surged from 12.0% to 66.6% (+54.6pp), reflecting successful programme distribution and adoption of cleaner cooking technologies
  • Sole ownership rising sharply: Women's sole ownership of cooking stoves jumped from 3.5% to 39.7% and solar panels from 9.2% to 31.6%, indicating a significant shift toward women directly controlling these productive assets
  • Solar panels growing steadily: Household access to solar panels increased from 53.4% to 64.7% (+11.3pp), with the growth driven primarily by sole ownership rather than joint ownership — suggesting women are acquiring panels independently
  • Joint ownership declining: For both assets, joint ownership decreased (solar 35.6%→28.8%, stoves 8.1%→26.3% for stoves it rose from very low base) while sole ownership surged — women are moving from shared to independent asset control
  • Target exceeded: The combined indicator (1220b) exceeded its 80.0% target at 83.1%, driven particularly by the massive uptake of cooking stoves which reduces women's time burden on fuel collection and cooking while cutting indoor air pollution

Forestry

Forest Management Knowledge & Conservation — Pre & Post Test Results

A. Knowledge

Forest Management Knowledge — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Training Assessment

Indicator 1130a: Percentage and Number of women and men with adequate practical knowledge in forest management and conservation (disaggregated by sex)

Baseline
50.0%
Target
68.0%
Midline Result
94.7%
Change
+44.7pp
Status
EXCEEDED

Forest Ecology & Rights — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Performance (%)

Forest Ecosystems: 56.6%→91.5% (+34.9pp). Biodiversity: 85.7%→97.6% (+11.9pp). Community Forestry: 86.6%→97.7% (+11.1pp). Forest Rights: 67.4%→95.0% (+27.6pp). Conservation Principles: 67.2%→97.8% (+30.6pp). Tree Species: 82.6%→97.5% (+14.9pp).

Management & Conservation — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Performance (%)

Policy & Sustainability — Pre-Test vs Post-Test Performance (%)

Forest Policy: 65.6%→95.3% (+29.7pp). Climate Adaptation: 74.7%→96.6% (+21.9pp). Community Governance: 96.9%→99.6% (+2.7pp). Benefit Sharing: 89.6%→98.6% (+9.0pp). Ecosystem Services: 88.5%→99.7% (+11.2pp). Agroforestry: 77.7%→97.9% (+20.2pp). Monitoring & Evaluation: 76.7%→97.4% (+20.7pp).

Key Insights — Forest Management Knowledge

  • Target massively exceeded: Knowledge levels reached 94.7% against a 68.0% target (+44.7pp from baseline), demonstrating the training programme's exceptional effectiveness in building forest management capacity
  • Largest gains in foundational topics: Forest Ecosystems (+34.9pp), Conservation Principles (+30.6pp), and Forest Rights (+27.6pp) saw the biggest improvements — areas where baseline knowledge was lowest, indicating the training successfully addressed critical gaps
  • Near-universal mastery achieved: Post-test scores exceeded 95% across 17 of 20 knowledge areas, with Carbon Sequestration (99.2%), Wildlife Corridors (99.2%), Ecosystem Services (99.7%), and Community Governance (99.6%) approaching perfect scores
  • Policy understanding transformed: Forest Policy knowledge jumped from 65.6% to 95.3% (+29.7pp) and Climate Adaptation from 74.7% to 96.6% (+21.9pp), equipping participants to engage meaningfully in forest governance and policy advocacy
  • Practical skills strengthened: Management Plans (68.9%→83.4%), Sustainable Harvesting (69.6%→95.7%), and Reforestation (67.0%→92.0%) all showed strong gains, translating knowledge into actionable forest management capabilities

B. Functionality

Community Forest Conservation Groups — Forest Functionality Assessment: Baseline to Midline Analysis

Indicator 1100a: Area (hectares) under rehabilitation using gender responsive nature-based solutions for climate mitigation and adaptation with biodiversity benefits, disaggregated by terrestrial, aquatic and coastal ecosystem

Baseline
0
Target
123.8 ha
Midline Result
63 ha
Change
+63 ha
Progress
50.9% of Target

Indicator 1130b: Percentage and Number of community forest conservation groups that are functional

Baseline
53.5%
Target
70.0%
Midline Result
85.7%
Achievement
+32.2pp
Status
EXCEEDED
A. OVERVIEW & FUNCTIONALITY SCORES

Functionality Assessment Summary

Overall group functionality improved significantly from baseline (n=43) to midline (n=28). Groups meeting the ≥60% threshold increased from 81.4% to 96.4% (+15.0pp). Groups meeting the ≥70% threshold surged from 53.5% to 85.7% (+32.2pp). Overall score improved from 70.6 to 84.2 (+13.6pp), with the strongest gains in Gender domain (+52.6pp).

Functionality Threshold — Groups Meeting ≥60% & ≥70% Benchmarks
Groups meeting ≥60% threshold: 81.4%→96.4% (+15.0pp). Groups meeting ≥70% threshold: 53.5%→85.7% (+32.2pp). Strong upward trajectory across both benchmarks.
Domain Scores — Management, Gender & Effectiveness
Management: 78.6%→78.0% (−0.6pp). Gender: 32.9%→85.5% (+52.6pp — strongest gain). Effectiveness: 72.0%→90.1% (+18.1pp). Overall: 70.6%→84.2% (+13.6pp).
Functionality Score Distribution — Average, Max & Min (Baseline n=43, Midline n=28)
Average score improved from 70.6 to 84.2 (+13.6). Maximum improved from 90.4 to 98.5. Minimum lifted from 37.0 to 46.7, indicating weakest groups are also improving.
B. GROUP CHARACTERISTICS

Group Profile Summary

Average group membership grew from 27 to 31 members, with female membership increasing from 19 to 23 (21% growth). Male membership averaged 7→8 members. Most groups operate in the 0-3 year range at baseline (34.9%) shifting slightly at midline (37.0%).

Average Group Membership — Male vs Female
Male: 7→8 (+14.3%). Female: 19→23 (+21.1%). Total: 27→31 (+14.8%). Female membership growing faster than male.
Group Affiliations — AMCOS, Beekeepers & Wellness
AMCOS: 42.5%→52.5% (+10.0pp). Beekeepers: 27.5%→35.0% (+7.5pp). Wellness: 15.0%→25.0% (+10.0pp). All affiliations growing.
Years of Operation — Distribution
Years distribution: 0-3 yrs: 34.9%→37.0%. 4-5 yrs: 30.2%→22.2%. 6-10 yrs: 20.9%→29.6%. 11+ yrs: 14.0%→11.1%.
Legal Registration Status
Registered groups increased from 72.5% to 85.0% (+12.5pp). Unregistered decreased 27.5%→15.0%. Formalization is progressing well.
Forest Size Distribution (Hectares)
Forest size distribution: 0 ha: 14.0%→0%. 1-5 ha: 30.2%→71.4%. 11-100 ha: 23.3%→7.1%. Midline shows concentration in 1-5 ha range.
Communities Near Forest — Distribution
More communities engaging: 7-10 communities increased 15.0%→20.0%. Groups with 1-3 communities declined 45.0%→35.0%, suggesting broader reach.
C. OBJECTIVES, RIGHTS & GOVERNANCE

Governance Improvements

Groups with fully written objectives already high at baseline: 95.3%→100%. Board roles well-defined & understood improved from 67.4% to 92.9%. Regular meetings with full quorum at 95.3%→96.4%. Women in significant leadership positions increased from 69.8% to 89.3%. Management practices (Agree+SA) remain strong above 82% across all 6 indicators.

Groups with Objectives in Written Format
Written objectives: No 2.3%→0%, Partially 2.3%→0%, Yes 95.3%→100%. Near-universal objective documentation.
Board/Committee Roles Definition & Understanding
Well-defined & understood: 67.4%→92.9% (+25.5pp). Not defined at all: 2.3%→3.6%. Governance clarity strengthening.
Meeting Regularity & Attendance
Regular/Full quorum: 95.3%→96.4% (+1.1pp). Occasional/Low: 2.3%→0%. Meeting participation consistently strong.
Women in Leadership Positions
Significant leadership: 69.8%→89.3% (+19.5pp). No leadership/no voice: 0%→0%. Strong female leadership in governance.
Management Practices — Agree + Strongly Agree (%)
Transparent elections: 95.3%→89.3%. Inclusive decisions: 97.7%→89.3%. Grievance mechanism: 97.7%→82.1%. Benefit sharing: 88.4%→92.9%. All indicators remain high (>82%).
D. TRAINING & ASSETS

Training & Resource Mobilization

Training coverage improved substantially: groups where "Most Members" received training grew from 11.6% to 71.4% (+59.8pp). Groups receiving no training dropped from 67.4% to 10.7%. Assets/inputs received increased from 18.6% to 78.6%. Key training topics include Climate Change & Forests (84%), CFM Concepts (52%), and Gender & Governance (52%).

Training Coverage — Member Participation Levels
Most members trained: 11.6%→71.4% (+59.8pp). No training: 67.4%→10.7% (-56.7pp). Training reach expanding dramatically.
Top Training Topics Received (%)
Climate Change & Forests: 57.1%→84.0%. CFM Concepts: 35.7%→52.0%. Forest Livelihoods: 21.4%→44.0%. Gender & Governance: 0%→52.0%.
Assets & Inputs Received
Groups receiving assets: 18.6%→78.6% (+60.0pp). Seedlings/nursery most common (63.6% at midline), followed by tools & equipment (77.3%) and water & irrigation (40.9%).
Asset Types Received by Groups (%)
Seedlings/Nursery: 4.7%→63.6%. Tools & Equipment: 9.3%→77.3%. Beekeeping: 2.3%→27.3%. Energy (Solar/Stoves): 0%→40.9%.
E. GENDER EQUALITY

Gender Equality Progress

Groups discussing gender equality increased from 23.3% to 96.4% (+73.1pp). Groups taking gender equality actions improved from 39.5% to 92.9%. Key topics discussed include equitable roles/responsibilities (96.3%), women's leadership & voice (81.5%), and resource sharing (55.6%). Gender action completion ("All actions") changed from 76.5% to 42.3%.

Groups Discussing Gender Equality
Yes: 23.3%→96.4% (+73.1pp). No: 76.7%→3.6%. Gender equality conversations now near-universal in forest groups.
Gender Equality Topics Discussed (%)
Equitable roles: 60.0%→96.3%. Resource sharing: 30.0%→55.6%. Women leadership & voice: 50.0%→81.5%. Strong gains across all topics.
Gender Equality Actions Taken
Yes (fully): 39.5%→92.9% (+53.4pp). No: 53.5%→7.1% (-46.4pp). Partially: 7.0%→0%. Groups moving decisively from discussion to action.
Gender Action Completion Level
All actions completed: 76.5%→42.3%. Many: 23.5%→50.0%. None: 0%→0%. Action completion shifting from "all" to "many" as scope of actions expanded.
F. EFFECTIVENESS & FOREST CONSERVATION

Conservation Outcomes

Forest condition rated "Good" varied across characteristics: Area 39.5%→42.9%, Biodiversity 39.5%→50.0%, Ecosystem Services 39.5%→39.3%. Threat levels were generally low: Fire (High) at 2.3%→0%, Charcoal (High) at 9.3%→7.1%. Income-generating groups increased from 46.5% to 53.6%. Agroforestry adoption grew from 55.8% to 60.7%.

Forest Condition — "Good" Rating Baseline vs Midline (%)
Area: 39.5%→42.9%. Volume/Biomass: 44.2%→25.0%. Regeneration: 44.2%→42.9%. Biodiversity: 39.5%→50.0%. Ecosystem Services: 39.5%→39.3%.
Forest Condition Breakdown — Baseline (Good / Medium / Poor)
Baseline: Good ratings range 39.5–44.2%. Medium ratings 25.6–37.2%. Poor ratings 23.3–30.2%. Volume/Biomass and Regeneration show highest Good ratings.
Forest Condition Breakdown — Midline (Good / Medium / Poor)
Midline: Good ratings range 25.0–50.0%. Medium ratings 35.7–50.0%. Poor ratings 7.1–25.0%. Biodiversity improved to 50.0% Good rating.
Forest Status Changes — Baseline (Decrease / No Change / Increase)
Baseline: Increase dominant across characteristics (55.8–65.9%). No Change ranges 16.3–27.9%. Decrease observed in 9.8–23.3% of groups.
Forest Status Changes — Midline (Decrease / No Change / Increase)
Midline: Decrease reduced (7.1–10.7%). No Change ranges 11.1–28.6%. Increase grew to 64.3–81.5%, showing strong positive conservation trajectory.
Forest Status — Perceived Increase Baseline vs Midline (%)
Groups perceiving increase: Area 60.5%→64.3%, Volume 60.5%→71.4%, Regeneration 55.8%→81.5%, Biodiversity 60.5%→81.5%, Ecosystem 65.9%→71.4%.
Forest Threats — "High" Level Baseline vs Midline (%)
Fire: 2.3%→0% (-2.3pp). Charcoal: 9.3%→7.1% (-2.2pp). Encroachment: 11.6%→3.6% (-8.0pp). Threat levels already low at baseline.
Threats Breakdown — Baseline (Low / Medium / High)
Baseline: High threats low overall. Fuelwood highest at 16.3%, Encroachment and Land Grabbing at 11.6%. Most threats predominantly Low level (65–93%).
Threats Breakdown — Midline (Low / Medium / High)
Midline: High threats further reduced (0–7.1%). Low threats dominant (71.4–89.3%). Medium levels stable. Continued low threat environment.
Threat Changes — Perceived Decrease Baseline vs Midline (%)
Groups perceiving threat decrease: Fire 48.8%→66.7%, Charcoal 48.8%→75.0%, Fuelwood 42.9%→67.9%. More groups reporting threat reduction at midline.
Income Generation & Agroforestry Adoption
Income-generating: 46.5%→53.6% (+7.1pp). Agroforestry adopted: 55.8%→60.7% (+4.9pp). Moderate economic growth observed.
Harvest Products — "Substantial" Amount (%)
NWFPs: 7.0%→3.6%. Food: 9.3%→3.6%. Woodfuel: 2.3%→0% (declining — positive conservation signal). Timber: 0%→0% (no extraction). Substantial harvesting minimal overall.
Agroforestry Practices & Objectives (%)
Top practices: Intercropping 83.3%→82.4%, Beekeeping 8.3%→23.5%, Alley Cropping 20.8%→0%. Top objectives: Biodiversity, soil fertility, income diversification.

Key Insights — Forest Group Functionality

  • Functionality threshold exceeded: Groups meeting ≥60% functionality rose from 81.4% to 96.4% (+15.0pp), while those at ≥70% surged from 53.5% to 85.7% (+32.2pp), demonstrating broad-based improvement
  • Gender domain transformed: Gender equality scores leapt from 32.9% to 85.5% (+52.6pp) — the strongest domain gain. Groups discussing gender equality grew from 23.3% to 96.4%, and those taking gender actions from 39.5% to 92.9%
  • Training reach dramatically expanded: Groups where "Most Members" were trained grew from 11.6% to 71.4% (+59.8pp). Asset receipt increased from 18.6% to 78.6% (+60.0pp), with tools/equipment (77.3%) and seedlings (63.6%) most common
  • Governance remains strong: Written objectives at 100%, board role clarity at 92.9% (+25.5pp from baseline), women in significant leadership at 89.3% (+19.5pp). All management practices above 82% agreement
  • Conservation trajectory positive: Groups perceiving biodiversity increase grew from 60.5% to 81.5%. High threats declined across all categories. Agroforestry adoption steady at 60.7%, with beekeeping emerging as a growing practice (8.3%→23.5%)

Seaweed

Seaweed Farming Knowledge & Group Assessment — Training Impact & Production Analysis

A. Knowledge

Adequate Knowledge by Threshold — Baseline vs Endline (%)

≥70 threshold: 67.4%→29.3% (−38.1pp). ≥60 threshold: 85.1%→66.5% (−18.6pp). Both thresholds show significant decline.

Adequate Knowledge (≥70) by Gender — Baseline vs Endline (%)

Female: 67.4%→28.8% (−38.6pp). Male: 67.6%→32.4% (−35.2pp). Both genders declined significantly.

Average Standardized Score by Gender — Baseline vs Endline

Female: 72.8→64.6 (−8.2). Male: 72.6→65.2 (−7.4). Overall: 72.7→64.7 (−8.0). Males slightly higher at endline.

Adequate Knowledge (≥60) by Gender — Baseline vs Endline (%)

Female: 85.5%→65.1% (−20.4pp). Male: 83.8%→76.5% (−7.3pp). Males retained more knowledge at ≥60 threshold.

Question-by-Question Analysis — Baseline vs Endline (% Correct)

# Baseline Endline Diff (E-B)
Q1. Three broad seaweed groups92.6%70.7%−21.8%
Q2. Seaweed group grown in Kenya52.6%4.9%−47.6%
Q3. Two seaweed varieties in Kwale98.3%97.3%−0.9%
Q4. Potential uses for seaweed50.9%27.4%−23.5%
Q5. Recommended farm has 10 plots74.3%46.8%−27.5%
Q6. Common seaweed farming methods7.4%3.4%−4.0%
Q7. Off-bottom method inputs96.6%81.4%−15.2%
Q8. Healthy seaweed characteristics28.6%58.6%+30.0%
Q9. Primary method to attach seaweed seedlings95.4%97.3%+1.9%
Q10. Statements regarding the process78.3%70.7%−7.6%
Q11. Routine farm management72.0%64.6%−7.4%
Q12. Steps involved in harvesting53.7%34.2%−19.5%
Q13. Disease affects seaweed96.6%97.0%+0.4%
Q14. Methods to control seaweed40.6%46.4%+5.8%
Q15. Drying seaweed on mats98.3%92.0%−6.3%
Q16. Dried seaweed on ground74.9%58.6%−16.3%
Q17. Seaweed farming can only be done in sea97.1%97.7%+0.6%
Q18. Primary method to attach seaweed94.3%99.2%+5.0%
Q19. Common pest that affects seaweed65.7%69.6%+3.9%
Q20. Seaweed helps in reducing ocean acidification88.0%54.0%−34.0%
Q21. Most common harvesting method97.1%99.6%+2.5%
Q22. Seaweed should not be used as a biofertilizer46.9%52.1%+5.2%

Top Gains — Questions with Largest Improvement

Q8 Healthy seaweed characteristics (+30.0pp) was the standout improvement. Q14 Methods to control (+5.8pp) and Q22 Biofertilizer (+5.2pp) also improved.

Largest Declines — Questions with Biggest Drop

Q2 Seaweed grown in Kenya (−47.6pp) had the steepest decline. Q20 Ocean acidification (−34.0pp) and Q5 Farm plots (−27.5pp) also dropped sharply.

All 22 Questions — Baseline vs Endline (% Correct)

Visual comparison across all 22 knowledge questions. Green bars above baseline indicate improvement; red bars below indicate decline.

Key Insights — Seaweed Knowledge Assessment

  • Significant knowledge decline: Adequate knowledge (≥70) dropped from 67.4% to 29.3% (−38.1pp), far below the 68.0% target. This represents a critical area requiring urgent programme attention and retraining
  • Gender patterns similar: Female knowledge declined from 67.4% to 28.8% (−38.6pp) while males dropped from 67.6% to 32.4% (−35.2pp). Both genders show comparable decline, though males retained slightly more knowledge
  • Steepest question declines: Q2 (Seaweed group grown in Kenya) dropped 47.6pp to only 4.9% correct, Q20 (Ocean acidification) fell 34.0pp, and Q5 (Recommended farm plots) declined 27.5pp — these specific topics need targeted reinforcement
  • Notable improvements exist: Q8 (Healthy seaweed characteristics) improved by +30.0pp, and Q18 (Primary attachment method) gained +5.0pp, demonstrating that practical/hands-on knowledge was better retained than theoretical knowledge
  • Average scores declined moderately: Overall average dropped from 72.7 to 64.7 (−8.0 points), with the ≥60 threshold still showing 66.5% adequacy, suggesting foundational knowledge persists but advanced recall has weakened considerably

B. Assessment

Seaweed Group Assessment — Production, Value Addition & Commercialization | 19 Groups | 610 Women

Indicator 1120b: Percentage and Number of targeted women with adequate resources to undertake regenerative and sustainable seaweed production, value addition and commercialization (disaggregated by type of activity)

Groups Assessed
19 Groups
Baseline
0%
Target
75%
Midline Result
34%
Status
ON TRACK

A. GROUP PROFILE

94.7% of groups are women-led with formal leadership. Most meet weekly (73.7%). Active female members total 610 across 19 groups (avg. 32.1 per group).

Groups by Year of Establishment

Women-Led Groups

Meeting Frequency

Active Members — Female vs Male

B. INVOLVEMENT IN SEAWEED ACTIVITIES

89.5% actively involved. Production (89.5%) and drying (63.2%) most common. 63.2% began from 2023 onwards. Market opportunity (47.4%) is the top motivator.

Activity Types Undertaken

Year Started Seaweed Activities

Motivation to Engage

Number of Ropes Owned

C. SEAWEED PRODUCTION & SUSTAINABILITY

89.5% apply regenerative practices. Off-bottom method dominates (78.9%). 78.9% report improved yield. Total harvest: 96,086 kg across groups.

Production Methods Used

Sustainable Practices Adoption

Challenges in Sustainable Practices

Yield/Quality Changes

Seaweed Produced in Last Harvest (kg)

Production Records Keeping

D. ACCESS TO INPUTS AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT

All groups received ropes and safety kits (100%). 89.5% received seedlings. Only 15.8% found inputs sufficient. All received seaweed farming training.

Inputs Received (by Type)

Input Adequacy Assessment

Technical Training Received

Additional Support Needs

E. COMMERCIALIZATION AND MARKET ACCESS

47.4% selling seaweed products. Dried seaweed (100% of sellers) and soap (55.6%) most common. Average price: KES 81.1/kg. Only 10% have branding strategies.

Groups Selling Seaweed Products

Products Sold by Type

Market Types Used

Commercialization Challenges

Seaweed Price Distribution (KES/kg)

Impact on Group Income

F. PARTNERSHIPS, SUSTAINABILITY & FUTURE PLANS

NGOs and government are key partners (63.2% each). Only one group has formal agreement with buyers (5.3%). Future plans focus on value addition and commercial expansion.

Partners Involved

Future Plans

Critical Needs to Scale

Formal Agreements with Buyers

SUMMARY, INSIGHTS & RECOMMENDATIONS

Summary

The assessment of 19 seaweed groups reveals a vibrant, women-led movement. 94.7% are women-led, meeting regularly (73.7% weekly). Production (89.5%) and drying (63.2%) dominate, but value addition (26.3%) and marketing (10.5%) remain underdeveloped. 89.5% apply regenerative practices, yet 94.7% face challenges. Only 47.4% are selling, and just one group has a formal buyer agreement.

Input adequacy is a major concern — only 15.8% feel inputs were sufficient. Training has been heavily technical; business, marketing, and quality control support reaches fewer groups. Groups aspire to expand operations with better tools, training, infrastructure, and market access.

Key Insights

  • High engagement, low adequacy: Nearly all received support but only a minority found it sufficient
  • Economic diversification:Seaweed farming catalyses broader economic activity
  • Commercialization bottleneck: Less than half are selling, and few have formal market linkages
  • Outliers: One group sold 27,000 kg; another has zero production despite having ropes

Recommendations

  • Strengthen market linkages: Facilitate formal contracts with buyers and cooperatives
  • Expand business training: Include pricing, negotiation, and entrepreneurship modules
  • Improve input distribution: Ensure equitable, sufficient supply of tools and materials
  • Invest in infrastructure: Drying sheds, storage facilities, and transport solutions

Key Insights — Seaweed Group Assessment

  • Functionality growing from zero: Moving from 0% baseline to 34% midline shows meaningful progress. The 75% target remains ambitious but achievable with continued investment in commercialization and buyer linkages
  • Production capacity established: 89.5% of groups apply sustainable practices, with total harvest of 96,086 kg. However, wide variation exists — from zero production to 31,000 kg in a single group
  • Commercialization is the critical gap: Only 47.4% are selling, average price is KES 81.1/kg, and just one group has a formal buyer agreement. This is the primary barrier to reaching the 75% functionality target
  • Women-led momentum: 94.7% women-led groups with 610 active female members demonstrate strong gender inclusion and organizational capacity across all 19 assessed groups
  • Input sufficiency gap: While 100% received ropes and safety kits, only 15.8% consider inputs sufficient. Insufficient inputs (84.2%) limit production scaling and constrain group functionality scores

SEAWEED FARMERS PROFILING & CATEGORIZATION

Farmers are categorized by Ropes in Ocean into 5 tiers. Each tier has a tailored support strategy.

⭐ Large Scale
300+ ropes in ocean
Support: Confidence to support
Support equals requested (meets ≥350 ropes target)
⭐ Upper Medium
200–299 ropes in ocean
Support: Given as requested
Meets target of ≥350 ropes
⭐ Lower Medium
100–199 ropes in ocean
Support: To reach 350 ropes
Focus on moving home ropes to ocean
⭐ Upper Small
50–99 ropes in ocean
Support: To reach 150 ropes
Focus on moving home ropes to ocean
⭐ Lower Small
<50 ropes in ocean
Priority: Move from home to ocean
Limited support to reach 50 ropes

Mangrove

Mangrove Restoration Knowledge & Resource Adequacy — Training Impact & Conservation Readiness

A. Knowledge

Mangrove Restoration Knowledge — Pre & Post Training Test Results

Indicator 1110a: Percentage and Number of women and men with adequate practical knowledge on gender-responsive and equitable mangrove restoration and conservation (disaggregated by sex)

Baseline (All)
36.9%
F: 34.7% | M: 38.2%
Target (All)
57.0%
F: 57.0% | M: 57.0%
Midline (All)
81.9%
F: 87.4% | M: 80.2%
Achievement
+45.0pp
Status
EXCEEDED
ADEQUATE KNOWLEDGE — BY LOCATION & GENDER

Knowledge Assessment Summary — Mangrove Restoration Training

Overall adequate knowledge improved dramatically from 36.9% to 81.5% (+44.6pp). By location, Kilifi showed the strongest gain (+59.0pp, from 21.1% to 80.1%), while Kwale improved from 53.7% to 83.1% (+29.4pp). By gender, females outperformed males at post-test (87.4% vs 80.2%), with females gaining +52.7pp compared to males' +42.0pp — demonstrating particularly strong knowledge uptake among women participants.

Overall Knowledge Gain
+44.6pp
36.9% → 81.5%
Kilifi County
+59.0pp
21.1% → 80.1%
Male Knowledge Gain
+42.0pp
38.2% → 80.2%
Female Knowledge Gain
+52.7pp
34.7% → 87.4%
Adequate Knowledge by Location — Pre-Test vs Post-Test (%)
Kilifi: 21.1%→80.1% (+59.0pp — strongest location gain). Kwale: 53.7%→83.1% (+29.4pp). Overall: 36.9%→81.5% (+44.6pp). Kilifi closed the gap with Kwale significantly.
Adequate Knowledge by Gender — Pre-Test vs Post-Test (%)
Male: 38.2%→80.2% (+42.0pp). Female: 34.7%→87.4% (+52.7pp — strongest gender gain). Overall: 36.9%→81.9% (+45.0pp). Women outperformed men at post-test by 7.2pp.

Key Insights — Mangrove Restoration Knowledge

  • Target exceeded significantly: Indicator 1110a exceeded the 57.0% target by +24.9pp, reaching 81.9% overall — demonstrating highly effective training delivery
  • Gender equity achievement: Women outperformed men at post-test (87.4% vs 80.2%), with a stronger gain of +52.7pp vs +42.0pp — training especially empowered female participants
  • Kilifi transformation: Kilifi county showed the strongest improvement (+59.0pp, from 21.1% to 80.1%), starting from the lowest baseline and nearly closing the gap with Kwale
  • Universal improvement: Both counties exceeded 80% post-test, confirming effective knowledge transfer across diverse locations and population groups

B. Resources

Mangrove Restoration Resources — Adequacy Assessment by Gender

Indicator 1110b: Percentage and Number of women and men with adequate resources to undertake mangrove restoration and conservation (disaggregated by sex)

Avg Score
50.6%
F: 51.3 | M: 48.6
Baseline
0%
Target
70%
Midline (≥60)
52%
F: 54.3% | M: 47.6%
Status
ON TRACK
Resource Adequacy — Threshold Analysis

Threshold Performance Summary

Members were scored on resource adequacy across Training, Seedlings, Materials, Sites, Financial Resources, Human Resources, and Overall perceptions. Scores at the ≥60 threshold show 54.3% female vs 47.6% male (52.0% overall). At the ≥40 threshold, 81.8% female vs 83.5% male (82.1% overall), indicating most members meet a basic adequacy level. The average score is 50.6 (Female: 51.3, Male: 48.6).

Threshold Attainment by Gender
At the ≥60 threshold, women outperform men (54.3% vs 47.6%). Gap narrows at ≥40 where men slightly lead (83.5% vs 81.8%).
Average Scores by Gender
Average resource adequacy score: Female 51.3, Male 48.6, Overall 50.6 — women consistently score higher across domains.
A. TRAINING

Training Summary

27.5% of members report training opportunities are "always available" (Female 27.5%, Male 36.2%, All 27.5%). Sufficiency of knowledge is rated "mostly sufficient" by 65.6% (Female 65.6%, Male 32.4%, All 60.8%). The training received is perceived as "very good" by 32.6% (Female 32.6%, Male 33.0%, All 32.6%) and "good" by 48.0%.

Availability of Training Opportunities (Past 12 Months)
Training is always available for 27.5% of all members; 36.2% of men vs 27.5% of women report consistent availability.
Sufficiency of Knowledge for Mangrove Restoration
65.6% of women feel mostly sufficient in their knowledge, but only 32.4% of men — a significant gender gap in perceived competence.
Perceived Quality of Training Received
80.6% rate training as very good or good overall. Minimal negative perception with only 1.2% rating it poor or very poor.
B. SEEDLINGS

Seedlings Summary

Seedlings/propagules are "always available" for only 19.9% of members (Female 19.4%, Male 21.4%). 56.1% report quantities are "mostly sufficient" for planned restoration. Quality and suitability are rated "good" by 50.0% and "very good" by 26.3%. However, 2.9% rate quality as poor, indicating some supply chain issues.

Availability of Mangrove Seedlings/Propagules
Quantity Sufficiency for Planned Restoration
Quality & Suitability of Seedlings
C. MATERIALS

Materials Summary

Only 12.7% rate planting materials supply as "very reliable" and 30.4% as reliable. Tools and equipment are "not available at all" for 42.1% of members. Sufficiency of tools is rated "completely insufficient" by 35.2%. Condition of tools is "very poor (often broken/unusable)" for 40.9% — a critical gap in material resources.

Reliability of Planting Materials Supply
Availability of Tools and Equipment
Sufficiency of Tools and Equipment
Condition and Quality of Tools
D. SITES

Sites Summary

Restoration sites are "easily accessible" for 24.0% and "mostly accessible" for 28.8%. Security of permission is "fully secure" for 36.4%. Access or permission issues are "sometimes" experienced by 24.6% and "rarely" by 31.3% — suggesting moderate institutional barriers.

Accessibility of Mangrove Restoration Sites
Security of Permission or Right to Work
Frequency of Access/Permission Issues
E. FINANCIAL RESOURCES

Financial Resources Summary

Financial resources are "not available at all" for 39.8% of members. Sufficiency is rated "barely sufficient" by 26.9% and "completely insufficient" by 19.3%. Reliability of financial support is rated "unreliable" by 46.5% — the biggest resource gap identified.

Availability of Financial Resources
Sufficiency of Financial Resources
Reliability of Financial Support
F. HUMAN RESOURCES

Human Resources Summary

Effectiveness in organizing activities is rated "very effective" by 31.1% and "mostly effective" by 34.3%. Time availability is "fully sufficient" for 27.1%. Labour reliability is rated "very reliable" by 32.2% and "somewhat reliable" by 43.7%.

Effectiveness in Organizing Activities
Availability of Time for Activities
Reliability of Group Labour
G. OVERALL

Overall Summary

Perceived strength of NGO/government support: 29.8% rate as "strong support" and 36.0% as "moderate support." Adequacy of all resources: only 12.3% rate as "fully adequate" and 21.6% as "mostly adequate." 11.1% rate resources as "completely inadequate" — highlighting the need for sustained investment.

Perceived Strength of NGO/Government Support
Adequacy of All Resources for Mangrove Restoration

Key Insights — Mangrove Resource Adequacy

  • Progress from zero baseline: Moving from 0% baseline to 52% at the ≥60 threshold shows meaningful resource mobilization, but the 70% target requires continued investment
  • Financial resources are the critical gap: 39.8% have no financial resources at all, with 46.5% rating support as unreliable — the weakest domain across all categories
  • Tools and equipment crisis: 42.1% have no tools at all, and 40.9% of existing tools are in very poor condition — urgent procurement and maintenance needed
  • Training quality is a strength: 80.6% rate training as very good or good, with 65.6% of women feeling knowledge is mostly sufficient
  • Gender equity in resources: Women consistently outperform men at the ≥60 threshold (54.3% vs 47.6%), suggesting women are more effectively utilizing available resources

Schools

Schools Programme — Access to Clean Water, Water Infrastructure & 4K Clubs
Schools Assessed
65 Schools
Counties
Kwale & Kilifi
Period
Baseline to Midline

Indicator 1310c Performance

The percentage of targeted schools with access to sustainable clean water solutions exceeded the 60% target, rising from 23.4% at baseline to 62.5% at midline — a gain of +39.1pp. This represents a significant achievement, demonstrating that COSME interventions in water infrastructure, rainwater harvesting, and school-based water management are delivering measurable impact.

Indicator Baseline Midline Target Change Status
OUTPUT LEVEL — Schools Programme (1310-series)
1310c. Percentage of targeted schools with access to sustainable clean water solutions 23.4% 62.5% 60% +39.1pp EXCEEDED

A. Access to Clean Water: Baseline vs. Midline Comparison

Between the Baseline (September 2023) and Midline (July 2025) assessments

Borehole Access
52.3%
↑ from 44.2%
Rainwater Harvesting
27.7%
↑ from 13.0%
Water Tanks
9.2%
↑ from 2.6%
Piped Water Access
35.4%
↑ from 31.2%
Year-round Availability
55.4%
↑ from 49.4%
Indicator 1310c (Composite)
62.5%
↑ from 23.4%

Narrative Summary

Between the Baseline (September 2023) and Midline (July 2025) assessments, several indicators related to access to clean water in schools showed notable changes:

Water Source Improvements:

  • Borehole access increased from 44.2% to 52.3%
  • Rainwater harvesting rose from 13.0% to 27.7%
  • Water tanks usage increased significantly from 2.6% to 9.2%
  • Piped water access improved slightly from 31.2% to 35.4%
  • River and pond usage decreased, indicating a shift away from unsafe sources

Ownership of Water Infrastructure:

  • Schools with private ownership of water infrastructure increased from 63.6% to 81.5%
  • Public/communal ownership dropped from 36.4% to 15.4%

Additional Indicators:

  • Schools reporting year-round availability rose from 49.4% to 55.4%
  • Schools reporting clean/safe drinking water slightly decreased from 81.2% to 78.5%
  • Functional rainwater harvesting systems increased from 37.7% to 53.8%
  • The percentage of schools with sustainable clean water solutions rose from 23.4% to 62.5% (Indicator 1310c)
Water Source: Baseline vs Midline (%)
Infrastructure Ownership: Baseline vs Midline (%)
Key Indicators: Baseline vs Midline (%)
Indicator 1310c — Sustainable Clean Water Solutions (%)

General Insights & Trends

  • Clear trend toward improved infrastructure and safer water sources, particularly through increased borehole and rainwater harvesting systems
  • Shift from public to private ownership suggests greater school-level control and possibly better maintenance
  • Despite infrastructure gains, the perception of water safety declined slightly, which may reflect increased awareness or persistent quality issues

Recommendations

  • Strengthen water quality monitoring to address concerns about safety
  • Continue investing in rainwater harvesting and borehole development, especially in areas with limited piped water
  • Provide technical support and training for maintenance of water systems to ensure long-term sustainability
  • Investigate reasons behind the decline in perceived water safety despite infrastructure improvements

B. Water in Schools: Midline Results

Midline data from sections A, B, and C

Schools Received Tanks & Gutters
83%
Tanks Installed
34%
Low uptake
Gutters Installed
32%
Low uptake
Solvatten Kits Used
49%
14% partially used
Maintenance Budget
20%
46% partially
Hygiene Training Programs
74%

Narrative Summary

COSME Project Interventions:

  • 83% of schools received water tanks and gutters
  • However, only 34% of schools installed tanks, and 32% installed gutters
  • Usage rates were lower: 35% used tanks, 27% used gutters
  • Only 30% of schools had tanks and gutters connected for rainwater harvesting

Solvatten Kits:

  • All 65 schools received kits
  • 49% used them, 14% partially used, and 37% did not use them

Sustainability Measures:

  • 20% of schools had budgets for maintenance; 46% partially
  • 57% had trained staff/students managing systems
  • 65% conducted repairs/maintenance in the past year
  • 74% had hygiene training programs for students
  • 74% involved community members in oversight
  • 63% had income-generating activities linked to water use
Infrastructure: Received vs Installed vs Used (%)
Solvatten Kit Usage (%)
Sustainability Measures (%)
Tank & Gutter Pipeline: Received → Installed → Used (%)

General Insights & Patterns

  • While distribution of infrastructure was high, installation and usage lagged, suggesting barriers beyond access — likely financial or technical
  • Solvatten kits had better uptake than tanks/gutters, possibly due to ease of use
  • Schools are increasingly engaging in sustainability practices, but funding remains a major constraint
  • Community involvement and student training are strong, which bodes well for long-term impact

Recommendations

  • Provide installation support and technical assistance post-distribution
  • Develop maintenance funding models, possibly through community partnerships or government support
  • Expand training programs for staff and students to improve system management
  • Monitor and evaluate usage of distributed resources to ensure effectiveness

C. 4K Clubs: Midline Results

4K Club Establishment, Training & Support, and Environmental Initiatives

Schools with 4K Clubs
97%
3% had two clubs
Total Members
5,065
2,917 girls · 2,148 boys
Materials Received
92%
6% partially
Curriculum Completed ~50%
66%
14% almost, 17% less
Environmental Initiatives
81.5%
of clubs initiated activities
Patrons Agree: Adequate Training
85%

Narrative Summary

4K Club Establishment:

  • 97% of schools had at least one club; 3% had two
  • Total members: 2,917 girls and 2,148 boys

Training & Support:

  • 85% agreed patrons received adequate training
  • 88% agreed patrons actively support clubs
  • 85% agreed training materials are adequate
  • 89% agreed club members are committed
  • 89% agreed clubs are functional and active

Material Distribution:

  • 92% of schools received materials; 6% partially
  • Beneficiaries: 2,153 girls, 1,632 boys

Curriculum Delivery:

  • 66% of schools completed around half
  • 14% almost completed, 17% less than half

Environmental Initiatives:

  • 81.5% of clubs initiated activities
  • Most common: Tree planting (38), Farming (32), Environmental conservation (9)
Training & Support Agreement (%)
4K Club Members: Gender Split
Environmental Initiatives by Type
Curriculum Delivery Progress

General Insights & Patterns

  • 4K Clubs are widely adopted and well-supported, with high levels of engagement
  • Girls outnumber boys in club participation and material benefit
  • Environmental initiatives are diverse and impactful, with strong emphasis on sustainability
  • Curriculum delivery is progressing but needs acceleration to reach full completion

Recommendations

  • Support full curriculum implementation through targeted follow-ups and resources
  • Encourage gender-balanced participation, especially for boys
  • Expand environmental initiative guidance to help clubs diversify and deepen impact
  • Continue investing in training and materials, which are clearly valued and effective

Overall Insights

Cross-cutting findings across Schools Programme components

Financial Limitations Dominate

The most recurring theme is lack of financial resources. Schools frequently cited "cash" or "lack of funding" as the primary barrier to installing or completing water systems. This includes costs for tanks, gutters, plinths, and repairs.

Infrastructure Gaps

Many schools lack basic infrastructure such as storage tanks, gutters, or functional roofs for rainwater harvesting. Even when materials are provided, installation is often delayed due to lack of funds or technical support.

Security and Vandalism

Several schools face insecurity issues, including theft and vandalism of water infrastructure. The absence of perimeter fencing exacerbates these problems.

Community Needs and Shared Use

In some cases, water systems are shared with the local community, creating strain on limited resources and complicating maintenance responsibilities.

Environmental and Logistical Challenges

Schools in dry regions or with unreliable water sources struggle more, even when infrastructure is present. This underscores the need for context-specific solutions.

Positive Outcomes

  • A few schools reported successful installations that improved irrigation and water availability.
  • A few schools report successful irrigation and crop watering, often linked to community or parental support.
  • Some schools express gratitude for support received, especially when water tanks help mitigate unreliable piped water.

Environmental Initiatives Engagement

Widespread Engagement: A total of 65 schools reported environmental initiatives, indicating strong engagement from youth clubs in environmental action.

Dominant Themes:

  • Tree Planting is the most common initiative, often linked to environmental conservation and climate resilience
  • Farming, including vegetable gardens and crop cultivation, is a close second

Emerging Practices:

  • Waste Management and Water Conservation are less frequently mentioned but represent growing areas of interest
  • A few schools are exploring hygiene education, irrigation, and poultry farming

Key Challenges Identified

  • Financial Constraints: Many schools cite lack of funds as a barrier to installing, maintaining, or repairing water systems
  • Security Issues: Insecurity, including theft and vandalism, is a recurring theme
  • Environmental Factors: Dry regions and unreliable water sources exacerbate maintenance challenges
  • Infrastructure Gaps: Even when materials are provided, installation is delayed due to lack of technical expertise
  • Community Pressure: Shared use of water systems with the community increases strain
